title: High Speed CAN Communication Bus
shortDescription: Fault detected on the high-speed CAN (Controller Area Network) communication bus.
fullDescription: Code U0001 indicates a general fault on the high-speed CAN bus, which is the primary communication network connecting major vehicle modules such as the ECM, TCM, ABS, and instrument cluster. The high-speed CAN bus operates at 500 kbps and carries critical powertrain and chassis data. A bus fault can cause multiple module communication failures and widespread vehicle symptoms.
safetyImpact: Multiple vehicle systems may lose communication, potentially disabling critical functions like engine management, transmission control, and ABS.

causes.cause: Damaged CAN bus wiring (open, short, or shorted wires together)
A single module shorting down the entire bus
Corroded connector on the CAN bus circuit

symptoms: Multiple U-codes stored across various modules
Engine may not start or runs poorly

fixSteps.title: Scan all modules
fixSteps.instruction: Perform a full vehicle scan of all modules. A high-speed CAN bus fault will typically show U-codes in many modules simultaneously.

fixSteps.title: Measure CAN bus signals
fixSteps.instruction: Measure the CAN-High and CAN-Low voltage at the DLC (diagnostic connector). CAN-H should be approximately 3.5V and CAN-L approximately 1.5V with the key on. Termination resistance should be 60 ohms between CAN-H and CAN-L.

fixSteps.title: Isolate faulty module
fixSteps.instruction: If the bus is shorted, disconnect modules one at a time while monitoring CAN bus voltage. When the bus recovers, the last disconnected module is the culprit.

fixSteps.title: Repair wiring or replace module
fixSteps.instruction: Repair damaged CAN bus wiring or replace the module that is shorting the bus. Verify all modules communicate after repair.

faq.q: What is the CAN bus?
faq.a: The Controller Area Network (CAN) bus is a communication system that allows all vehicle electronic modules to share data over a pair of twisted wires, eliminating the need for individual wires between every module.

faq.q: Can a single bad module take down the entire CAN bus?
faq.a: Yes. If a module shorts the CAN bus wires, it can disrupt communication for all modules on that bus segment, causing widespread failures.

faq.q: Why are there so many U-codes at once?
faq.a: When the CAN bus has a fundamental fault, every module that depends on it will set a communication loss code for every other module it cannot reach, creating a cascade of U-codes.

title: High Speed CAN Communication Bus Performance
shortDescription: Performance issue detected on the high-speed CAN communication bus.
fullDescription: Code U0002 indicates a performance degradation on the high-speed CAN bus rather than a complete failure. The bus is still functioning but experiencing excessive errors, noise, or reduced bandwidth. This can cause intermittent communication issues between modules, resulting in sporadic symptoms that may be difficult to reproduce.
safetyImpact: Intermittent communication losses can cause unexpected behavior in safety systems like ABS and engine control.

causes.cause: Marginal wiring connection or chafed CAN bus wire
CAN bus termination resistance slightly out of spec

symptoms: Symptoms may appear and disappear randomly

fixSteps.title: Full system scan
fixSteps.instruction: Scan all modules for codes. Note which codes are current vs. history. A pattern of intermittent U-codes across multiple modules points to bus performance issues.

fixSteps.title: Inspect CAN bus wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Visually inspect accessible CAN bus wiring for chafing, pinching, or areas where the wires run near high-power cables or aftermarket equipment.

fixSteps.title: Monitor bus with oscilloscope
fixSteps.instruction: Connect an oscilloscope to the CAN-H and CAN-L lines. Look for noise, ringing, or abnormal signal patterns that indicate bus interference.

fixSteps.title: Repair or eliminate interference
fixSteps.instruction: Repair any damaged wiring, separate CAN wiring from interference sources, or disconnect aftermarket equipment that may be causing noise.

faq.q: Can aftermarket stereos cause CAN bus issues?
faq.a: Yes. Poorly installed aftermarket stereos, amplifiers, or lighting systems can introduce electrical noise that interferes with CAN bus communication.

faq.q: Why are the symptoms intermittent?
faq.a: CAN bus performance issues cause occasional lost messages rather than complete communication failure. The effects are seen as sporadic glitches rather than consistent failures.

faq.q: What is CAN bus termination?
faq.a: The CAN bus requires a 120-ohm termination resistor at each end of the bus. These prevent signal reflections. If one is missing or damaged, communication quality degrades.

title: High Speed CAN Communication Bus (+) Open
shortDescription: Open circuit detected on the CAN-High (+) wire of the high-speed CAN bus.
fullDescription: Code U0003 indicates that an open circuit has been detected on the CAN-High (CAN-H) wire of the high-speed CAN bus. The CAN bus requires both CAN-H and CAN-L wires to communicate. An open on CAN-H prevents proper differential signaling, though some limited communication may still occur on the remaining wire at reduced reliability.
safetyImpact: CAN bus communication is severely degraded. Multiple control modules may lose communication with each other.

causes.cause: Broken CAN-H wire in wiring harness
Disconnected or corroded connector with CAN-H pin
Physical damage to harness from collision or rodent chew
Failed module with internal CAN-H open

fixSteps.title: Verify CAN bus voltage
fixSteps.instruction: Measure CAN-H voltage at the OBD-II DLC pin 6. It should be approximately 3.5V with key on. If 0V or battery voltage, the CAN-H line has an issue.

fixSteps.title: Check termination resistance
fixSteps.instruction: Measure resistance between DLC pins 6 (CAN-H) and 14 (CAN-L) with ignition off. Should read approximately 60 ohms. Higher resistance suggests an open in the bus.

fixSteps.title: Locate the open
fixSteps.instruction: Using a wiring diagram, trace the CAN-H wire through the vehicle. Check connectors and sections where the harness is routed near potential damage points.

fixSteps.title: Repair wire
fixSteps.instruction: Splice and solder the broken CAN-H wire. Use proper automotive-grade wire and heat-shrink insulation. Verify the bus recovers and all modules communicate.

faq.q: What are the CAN-H and CAN-L wires?
faq.a: The CAN bus uses two wires: CAN-High and CAN-Low. Data is transmitted as a voltage difference between the two wires. This differential signaling provides noise immunity.

faq.q: Can rodents damage CAN bus wiring?
faq.a: Yes. Rodents frequently chew vehicle wiring, and CAN bus wires are a common target. Soy-based wire insulation used by some manufacturers is particularly attractive to rodents.

faq.q: Where are CAN bus wires located?
faq.a: CAN bus wires run throughout the vehicle, typically in the main wiring harness. They connect to all electronic modules, so the wires route through the engine bay, dashboard, and to each module location.

title: High Speed CAN Communication Bus (-) Open
shortDescription: Open circuit detected on the CAN-Low (-) wire of the high-speed CAN bus.
fullDescription: Code U0004 indicates an open circuit on the CAN-Low (CAN-L) wire of the high-speed CAN bus. Similar to a CAN-H open, this severely degrades bus communication. The CAN bus may still partially function on the remaining wire but with significantly reduced noise immunity and reliability.
safetyImpact: CAN bus communication is severely degraded, affecting multiple safety-critical control modules.

causes.cause: Harness damage from accident or service work

symptoms: Multiple U-codes stored in many modules
Intermittent or complete loss of module communication

fixSteps.title: Measure CAN-L voltage
fixSteps.instruction: Check CAN-L voltage at DLC pin 14. Should read approximately 1.5V with key on. Abnormal voltage indicates a CAN-L wire issue.

fixSteps.title: Check bus resistance
fixSteps.instruction: Measure between DLC pins 6 and 14 with key off. Should be 60 ohms. An abnormal reading points to a CAN-L open or short.

fixSteps.title: Trace and locate break
fixSteps.instruction: Follow the CAN-L wire from the DLC through the vehicle harness. Check at all connectors and known damage-prone areas.

fixSteps.title: Repair
fixSteps.instruction: Repair the broken CAN-L wire using solder and heat-shrink. Verify bus restoration by scanning all modules.

faq.q: Can the vehicle still run with one CAN wire broken?
faq.a: In some cases, the vehicle may still operate in a degraded mode with one wire open. However, communication will be unreliable and many systems will not function correctly.

faq.q: How do I find a broken wire in a wiring harness?
faq.a: Use a multimeter to check continuity in sections, working from the DLC outward. A tone generator and probe can also help locate breaks. Focus on areas where the harness bends or has been previously repaired.

faq.q: Can I use a regular wire to repair the CAN bus?
faq.a: Use the same gauge wire as the original. The CAN bus wires are typically twisted pair; maintaining the twist is important for noise rejection. Keep the repair as short as possible.

title: High Speed CAN Communication Bus (+) High
shortDescription: CAN-High wire voltage is stuck high or shorted to battery voltage.
fullDescription: Code U0005 indicates that the CAN-High wire is stuck at an abnormally high voltage level, typically shorted to battery voltage. Normal CAN-H voltage alternates between approximately 2.5V (recessive) and 3.5V (dominant). A short to battery voltage locks the bus in an invalid state, preventing communication.
safetyImpact: CAN bus communication is disabled. Multiple vehicle systems will not function.

causes.cause: CAN-H wire chafed and shorted to a power wire
Module with internal CAN-H short to battery

symptoms: All dashboard lights may be on or off

fixSteps.title: Verify CAN-H voltage
fixSteps.instruction: Measure voltage on DLC pin 6 (CAN-H). If it reads near battery voltage (12V+), the line is shorted to power.

fixSteps.title: Disconnect modules to isolate
fixSteps.instruction: Disconnect modules one at a time from the CAN bus while monitoring CAN-H voltage. When voltage drops to normal, the last disconnected module or its wiring contains the short.

fixSteps.title: Inspect wiring route
fixSteps.instruction: Once the section is identified, inspect the CAN-H wire route for chafing against power wires, connector damage, or improper aftermarket taps.

fixSteps.title: Repair short
fixSteps.instruction: Repair the short by separating and insulating the CAN-H wire from the power source. Verify all modules communicate after repair.

faq.q: Why can't my scanner connect at all?
faq.a: If the CAN bus is shorted to battery voltage, the OBD-II scanner cannot establish communication because the bus protocol requires proper voltage levels to function.

faq.q: Can this damage my vehicle's modules?
faq.a: A CAN-H short to battery voltage can potentially damage the CAN transceiver chips in connected modules if they are not adequately protected. Repair should be prompt.

faq.q: How do I know if a module caused the short or if it is wiring?
faq.a: If the voltage drops to normal when you disconnect a specific module, try reconnecting it. If the short returns, the module's internal CAN transceiver may have failed and is pulling the bus high.

title: High Speed CAN Communication Bus (-) Low
shortDescription: CAN-Low wire voltage is stuck low or shorted to ground.
fullDescription: Code U0006 indicates that the CAN-Low wire is stuck at ground or near-ground voltage. Normal CAN-L alternates between approximately 2.5V (recessive) and 1.5V (dominant). A short to ground prevents the differential signaling from working, disabling the CAN bus.
safetyImpact: CAN bus is non-functional. All modules on the high-speed bus cannot communicate.

causes.cause: CAN-L wire chafed and shorted to chassis ground
Module with internal CAN-L short to ground
Water-damaged connector shorting CAN-L to ground pin
Improper wiring repair bridging CAN-L to ground

fixSteps.title: Check CAN-L voltage
fixSteps.instruction: Measure CAN-L at DLC pin 14. If it reads 0V or very low, the line is shorted to ground.

fixSteps.title: Module isolation test
fixSteps.instruction: Systematically disconnect modules while checking if CAN-L voltage recovers. This identifies the circuit segment containing the short.

fixSteps.title: Locate short
fixSteps.instruction: In the identified segment, trace the CAN-L wire and look for contact with metal body parts, ground wires, or water-damaged connectors.

fixSteps.title: Repair
fixSteps.instruction: Insulate the CAN-L wire from ground contact. Dry and clean any water-damaged connectors. Replace module if it has an internal short.

faq.q: Is a CAN-L short to ground different from a CAN-H short?
faq.a: Yes. CAN-L normally operates at 1.5-2.5V, so a short to ground pulls it below normal range. CAN-H normally operates at 2.5-3.5V. Both types of shorts can disable the bus.

faq.q: Can water damage cause CAN bus shorts?
faq.a: Yes. Water entering a connector can create conductive paths between CAN bus pins and ground pins, effectively shorting the bus.

faq.q: How do I prevent CAN bus wiring damage?
faq.a: Ensure wire looms and grommets are intact, avoid routing aftermarket wiring near the CAN bus harness, and repair any water leaks that could affect connectors.

title: High Speed CAN Communication Bus (+) Low
shortDescription: CAN-High wire is stuck at abnormally low voltage or shorted to ground.
fullDescription: Code U0007 indicates the CAN-High wire is pulled low, either shorted to ground or to the CAN-Low wire. This prevents the CAN-H line from reaching its normal dominant voltage of 3.5V, effectively disabling differential signaling on the bus.
safetyImpact: CAN bus communication is disabled. Safety-critical modules cannot exchange data.

causes.cause: Module internal CAN transceiver pulling H line low
Connector damage shorting H to ground pin

symptoms: Vehicle may not start or run
All dashboard lights on or blank

fixSteps.title: Measure bus voltages
fixSteps.instruction: Check CAN-H (pin 6) and CAN-L (pin 14) at the DLC. If both read the same voltage, the wires may be shorted together.

fixSteps.title: Check for wire-to-wire short
fixSteps.instruction: With key off and modules disconnected, check resistance between CAN-H and CAN-L. Very low resistance (near 0 ohms) indicates the wires are touching.

fixSteps.title: Trace and isolate
fixSteps.instruction: Follow the CAN bus wiring looking for points where H and L wires may be damaged and contacting each other or ground.

fixSteps.title: Repair
fixSteps.instruction: Separate and insulate the damaged wires. Verify CAN-H and CAN-L voltages return to normal with all modules reconnected.

faq.q: Can the CAN-H and CAN-L wires short to each other?
faq.a: Yes. Since they run as a twisted pair in the harness, if the insulation is damaged, they can contact each other, effectively shorting the bus.

faq.q: What voltage should I see on CAN-H and CAN-L?
faq.a: With key on and engine off: CAN-H should be roughly 2.5-3.5V and CAN-L should be roughly 1.5-2.5V. Both lines should have some activity (voltage fluctuation).

faq.q: Can I test the CAN bus without a scan tool?
faq.a: Yes. A simple multimeter can check bus voltages and termination resistance at the DLC connector, which can identify many bus faults without needing scanner communication.

title: High Speed CAN Communication Bus (-) High
shortDescription: CAN-Low wire is stuck at abnormally high voltage or shorted to battery.
fullDescription: Code U0008 indicates the CAN-Low wire is stuck at an abnormally high voltage, typically shorted to battery voltage or to the CAN-High wire. The CAN-L line should never exceed 2.5V in normal operation. This condition prevents proper CAN bus differential signaling.
safetyImpact: CAN bus communication is disabled. All modules on the high-speed bus are non-communicative.

causes.cause: CAN-L wire chafed and touching power wire
Module with internal CAN-L driver fault
Connector pin damage bridging CAN-L to voltage source

symptoms: Dashboard may be completely dark or all lights on

fixSteps.title: Check CAN-L voltage
fixSteps.instruction: Measure CAN-L at DLC pin 14. If it reads near battery voltage or matches CAN-H exactly, there is a short.

fixSteps.title: Disconnect and isolate
fixSteps.instruction: Disconnect modules and segments of the CAN bus systematically to isolate where the short is originating.

fixSteps.title: Find and fix short
fixSteps.instruction: Locate the point where CAN-L is contacting a power source or CAN-H. Repair insulation and separate wires.

fixSteps.title: Verify repair
fixSteps.instruction: Reconnect all modules and verify proper CAN bus voltages and communication with a scanner.

faq.q: What is the normal voltage range for CAN-L?
faq.a: CAN-L normally operates between 1.5V (dominant/active) and 2.5V (recessive/idle). Any voltage significantly outside this range indicates a fault.

faq.q: Can I use a test light to check CAN bus wires?
faq.a: No. A test light can damage CAN bus transceivers by drawing too much current. Always use a high-impedance digital multimeter or oscilloscope.

faq.q: How many modules are on the high-speed CAN bus?
faq.a: Typically 10-20 modules depending on the vehicle. Major modules include ECM, TCM, ABS, airbag, instrument cluster, and body control module.

title: High Speed CAN Communication Bus Shorted Together
shortDescription: CAN-High and CAN-Low wires are shorted to each other.
fullDescription: Code U0009 indicates that the CAN-High and CAN-Low wires are shorted together. When both wires are at the same voltage, the differential signal that carries data is eliminated. No communication is possible on the bus while this short exists. This is often caused by physical wire damage or connector issues.
safetyImpact: Complete CAN bus failure. No module communication is possible.

causes.cause: Harness damage shorting CAN-H to CAN-L
Connector pin bridge between H and L
Improper repair or splice connecting both wires

fixSteps.title: Confirm short
fixSteps.instruction: Measure resistance between DLC pins 6 (CAN-H) and 14 (CAN-L) with key off and battery disconnected. Near 0 ohms confirms a direct short (normal is about 60 ohms).

fixSteps.title: Isolate section
fixSteps.instruction: Disconnect modules to isolate bus segments. Check resistance in each segment to find where the short exists.

fixSteps.title: Locate physical short
fixSteps.instruction: In the affected segment, inspect wiring for physical damage. Pay attention to harness bends, connector junctions, and areas of prior repair.

fixSteps.title: Separate and repair wires
fixSteps.instruction: Separate the shorted wires and repair insulation. Verify resistance returns to approximately 60 ohms and all modules communicate.

faq.q: Why is the resistance 60 ohms normally?
faq.a: The CAN bus has two 120-ohm termination resistors, one at each end. In parallel, they measure 60 ohms. If you read 120 ohms, one terminator is missing. If near 0 ohms, the lines are shorted together.

faq.q: Can a connector that got wet cause this?
faq.a: Yes. Water in a connector can create a conductive bridge between the CAN-H and CAN-L pins, effectively shorting the bus.

faq.q: Is this a difficult repair?
faq.a: Finding the location of the short can be time-consuming since the CAN bus runs throughout the entire vehicle. Once found, the actual wire repair is straightforward.

title: Medium Speed CAN Communication Bus
shortDescription: Fault detected on the medium-speed CAN communication bus.
fullDescription: Code U0010 indicates a general fault on the medium-speed CAN bus, which typically operates at 125 kbps or 250 kbps. The medium-speed bus typically connects body and comfort modules such as the HVAC, seat modules, door modules, and entertainment system. A fault on this bus affects convenience features but usually does not affect powertrain or safety-critical systems.
safetyImpact: Primarily affects comfort and convenience features. May affect exterior lighting on some vehicles.

causes.cause: Failed body-side module pulling down the bus
Connector corrosion on medium-speed bus segment

symptoms: Door locks, windows, or mirrors not responding
Multiple B-codes and U-codes in body modules

fixSteps.title: Scan all modules
fixSteps.instruction: Perform a full vehicle scan. Identify which modules are on the medium-speed bus and which are reporting communication faults.

fixSteps.title: Check bus voltages
fixSteps.instruction: Medium-speed CAN uses the same voltage levels as high-speed (CAN-H ~3.5V, CAN-L ~1.5V). Verify at the relevant connector.

fixSteps.title: Isolate faulty segment
fixSteps.instruction: Disconnect body modules one at a time while monitoring bus health. Identify the module or wiring segment causing the fault.

fixSteps.title: Repair
fixSteps.instruction: Repair wiring or replace the faulty module. Verify all body functions restore to normal.

faq.q: What is the difference between high-speed and medium-speed CAN?
faq.a: High-speed CAN (500 kbps) carries time-critical data for powertrain and safety systems. Medium-speed CAN (125-250 kbps) handles less time-critical body and comfort functions.

faq.q: Can a medium-speed bus fault affect the engine?
faq.a: Generally no. The engine and powertrain are on the high-speed bus. However, some vehicles use gateway modules that could relay errors between buses.

faq.q: Are the two CAN buses physically separate?
faq.a: Yes. They are separate sets of wires with their own termination resistors. They may be connected through a gateway module that translates messages between them.

title: Control Module Communication Bus Off
shortDescription: A control module has gone to bus-off state on the CAN network.
fullDescription: Code U0073 indicates that a control module has entered the CAN bus-off state. The CAN protocol includes an error management mechanism where a module that accumulates too many communication errors will disconnect itself from the bus (bus-off) to prevent it from disrupting other modules. This is a protective measure but results in that module being completely unreachable.
safetyImpact: The affected module is completely disconnected from the network and non-functional until the bus-off condition is resolved.

causes.cause: CAN bus wiring fault causing excessive errors for one module
Module with failing CAN transceiver generating errors
Bus noise or interference at one module's connection point
Module firmware bug causing bus-off condition

symptoms: Functions controlled by that module are all inoperative
U-code in other modules indicating lost communication with the affected module
Problem may resolve temporarily after ignition cycle

fixSteps.title: Identify affected module
fixSteps.instruction: Scan all modules. The module in bus-off state will not respond to the scanner. Other modules will have U-codes indicating they lost communication with the affected module.

fixSteps.title: Check module wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect the CAN bus wiring specifically at the affected module's connector and its connection to the main bus harness.

fixSteps.title: Test module CAN transceiver
fixSteps.instruction: With the module disconnected, check if the CAN bus is otherwise healthy. Then check the module's CAN pin resistance and behavior when reconnected.

fixSteps.title: Repair or replace
fixSteps.instruction: Repair wiring issues or replace the module if its CAN transceiver has failed. Verify the module stays on the bus after repair.

faq.q: What does bus-off mean?
faq.a: Bus-off is a CAN protocol safety feature. When a module detects too many communication errors, it disconnects itself from the bus to protect other modules from being affected by its errors.

faq.q: Will cycling the ignition fix it?
faq.a: Temporarily, possibly. When the module powers up again, it will attempt to rejoin the bus. However, if the underlying cause persists, it will accumulate errors and go bus-off again.

faq.q: Can a bad ground at one module cause bus-off?
faq.a: Yes. A poor ground can cause the module's CAN transceiver to operate at incorrect voltage levels, generating bus errors until it reaches the bus-off threshold.

title: Lost Communication with ECM/PCM
shortDescription: A module has lost communication with the Engine Control Module (ECM) or Powertrain Control Module (PCM).
fullDescription: Code U0100 indicates that one or more modules have lost communication with the Engine Control Module (ECM) or Powertrain Control Module (PCM). The ECM/PCM is the most critical module on the vehicle network, controlling fuel injection, ignition, and emissions. Other modules depend on ECM data for their own functions. This code can be set in any module that expects to receive data from the ECM.
safetyImpact: Engine may not run. If it does run, multiple systems relying on ECM data will not function properly.

causes.cause: CAN bus wiring fault between ECM and the rest of the network
ECM power supply or ground fault preventing the module from operating

symptoms: Multiple U-codes in other modules (TCM, ABS, BCM, etc.)
Instrument cluster not showing engine data (RPM, temperature)

fixSteps.title: Verify ECM communication
fixSteps.instruction: Attempt to communicate with the ECM directly using a scanner. If the ECM responds, the issue may be intermittent. If it does not respond, check power and ground.

fixSteps.title: Check ECM power and ground
fixSteps.instruction: Verify battery voltage at the ECM main power supply connector and verify all ground connections are clean and tight.

fixSteps.title: Inspect ECM CAN bus connection
fixSteps.instruction: Check the CAN bus wires at the ECM connector for damage. Verify continuity from the ECM CAN pins to the main bus harness.

fixSteps.title: Replace ECM if necessary
fixSteps.instruction: If power, ground, and wiring are all verified good and the ECM still does not communicate, replace the ECM. Program and configure to the vehicle.

faq.q: Can a bad battery cause U0100?
faq.a: Yes. If the battery voltage drops too low, the ECM may not have enough power to operate and communicate on the CAN bus.

faq.q: Why do so many other codes appear with U0100?
faq.a: The ECM broadcasts critical data (engine speed, temperature, vehicle speed) that many other modules need. When the ECM stops communicating, all dependent modules set their own lost-communication codes.

faq.q: Is this always an expensive repair?
faq.a: Not necessarily. Many U0100 cases are caused by wiring issues or poor connections that are inexpensive to repair. ECM replacement is less common.

title: Lost Communication with TCM
shortDescription: A module has lost communication with the Transmission Control Module (TCM).
fullDescription: Code U0101 indicates that one or more vehicle modules have lost communication with the Transmission Control Module (TCM). The TCM controls automatic transmission shift points, torque converter lockup, and gear selection. Loss of communication can result in the transmission entering a fail-safe mode with limited gear availability.
safetyImpact: Transmission may enter limp mode (single gear), limiting vehicle speed and acceleration. This can be dangerous in highway merging situations.

causes.cause: CAN bus wiring fault near TCM
Corroded TCM connector (especially on external-mount TCMs)

symptoms: Transmission stuck in one gear (limp mode)
U0101 code in ECM, BCM, or other modules

fixSteps.title: Attempt TCM communication
fixSteps.instruction: Try to communicate with the TCM using a scanner. If it does not respond, check for power and ground at the TCM connector.

fixSteps.title: Check TCM power supply
fixSteps.instruction: Locate the TCM (on or attached to the transmission on some vehicles, or under the dashboard on others). Check power supply voltage and grounds.

fixSteps.title: Inspect TCM connector
fixSteps.instruction: TCMs mounted on the transmission are exposed to heat, vibration, and moisture. Inspect the connector for corrosion, water intrusion, or heat damage.

fixSteps.title: Repair or replace
fixSteps.instruction: Repair wiring or connector issues. If the TCM itself has failed, replace and program it. Clear codes and verify proper shifting.

faq.q: What is transmission limp mode?
faq.a: Limp mode is a fail-safe condition where the transmission defaults to a single gear (usually 3rd or 4th) to allow the vehicle to be driven at reduced speed to a repair facility.

faq.q: Can I still drive in limp mode?
faq.a: Yes, but at reduced speed and power. Avoid highway driving as acceleration will be severely limited. Drive carefully to a repair shop.

faq.q: Is the TCM inside the transmission?
faq.a: On some vehicles, the TCM is mounted externally on the transmission case. On others, it is located inside the vehicle cabin under the dashboard or center console.

title: Lost Communication with Transfer Case Control Module
shortDescription: A module has lost communication with the transfer case control module.
fullDescription: Code U0102 indicates loss of communication with the transfer case control module on 4WD or AWD vehicles. The transfer case module controls the engagement and disengagement of four-wheel drive modes and distributes torque between the front and rear axles. Loss of communication can lock the transfer case in its current mode.
safetyImpact: Four-wheel drive selection may be unavailable or the system may be stuck in one mode, affecting vehicle handling.

causes.cause: CAN bus wiring fault near transfer case module

symptoms: 4WD indicator lights not working or flashing
Cannot switch between 2WD, 4WD, or AWD modes
Transfer case stuck in current mode

fixSteps.title: Scan for codes
fixSteps.instruction: Read all codes. Check if the transfer case module is communicating. Note if other modules also report communication losses.

fixSteps.title: Check module connections
fixSteps.instruction: Locate the transfer case control module (usually near the transfer case under the vehicle). Inspect connector for water, corrosion, and damage.

fixSteps.title: Test power and CAN bus
fixSteps.instruction: Verify power supply, ground, and CAN bus signal at the module connector.

fixSteps.title: Repair or replace
fixSteps.instruction: Repair wiring or connector issues. Replace the module if it has failed. Program and configure as needed.

faq.q: Can I still drive without the transfer case module communicating?
faq.a: The vehicle is usually drivable, but you cannot change 4WD modes. If stuck in 4WD-Lock on dry pavement, drive carefully to avoid drivetrain binding.

faq.q: Why does the module corrode so easily?
faq.a: Transfer case modules mounted under the vehicle are exposed to road spray, salt, mud, and debris. Their connectors are particularly vulnerable to water intrusion.

faq.q: Can a low battery affect the transfer case module?
faq.a: Yes. Transfer case motors draw significant current during mode changes. A weak battery can cause voltage drops that disrupt communication during shifting.

title: Lost Communication with Gear Shift Module
shortDescription: A module has lost communication with the gear shift control module.
fullDescription: Code U0103 indicates that communication has been lost with the gear shift module. On vehicles with electronic gear selectors (shift-by-wire), this module translates the driver's gear selection into electronic commands sent to the transmission. Loss of communication can prevent gear changes and may lock the transmission in park or its current gear.
safetyImpact: Vehicle may not be able to shift gears. If stuck in park, the vehicle cannot be moved. If stuck in drive, stopping the engine may be the only way to prevent movement.

causes.cause: CAN bus wiring damage near shifter
Spilled liquid in center console affecting module

symptoms: Gear indicator not showing current gear

fixSteps.title: Scan codes
fixSteps.instruction: Read all module codes. Check if the gear shift module is responding to the scanner.

fixSteps.title: Inspect shifter area
fixSteps.instruction: Remove the center console trim to access the gear shift module. Check for liquid damage, loose connectors, or visible wire damage.

fixSteps.title: Check module power and communication
fixSteps.instruction: Verify power supply, ground, and CAN bus signals at the gear shift module connector.

fixSteps.title: Replace module
fixSteps.instruction: If the module has failed, replace it. Some modules require programming. Clear codes and verify all gear selections work correctly.

faq.q: What is shift-by-wire?
faq.a: Shift-by-wire systems replace the mechanical cable between the gear selector and transmission with electronic signals. The shifter sends commands through a module over the CAN bus to the TCM.

faq.q: Can I move the vehicle if it is stuck in park?
faq.a: Many vehicles have an emergency shift release button or procedure. Check your owner's manual for the override method.

faq.q: Can a drink spill cause this?
faq.a: Yes. The gear shift module is often located in the center console area. Liquid spills can damage the module electronics or corrode its connector.

title: Lost Communication with Cruise Control Module
shortDescription: A module has lost communication with the cruise control module.
fullDescription: Code U0104 indicates that communication has been lost with the cruise control module. This module manages conventional cruise control and, on some vehicles, adaptive cruise control (ACC). Loss of communication disables cruise control functionality and may trigger related fault codes in the ECM or BCM.
safetyImpact: Cruise control is unavailable. No immediate safety hazard, but convenience is affected.

causes.cause: CAN bus wiring fault to cruise module

symptoms: Cruise control indicator light not illuminating

fixSteps.title: Scan for codes
fixSteps.instruction: Scan all modules. Check if the cruise control module is on the network. Some vehicles integrate cruise control into the ECM.

fixSteps.title: Check fuses and power
fixSteps.instruction: Verify the cruise control module fuse and power supply. Also check the clock spring (on vehicles with steering wheel cruise buttons).

fixSteps.title: Inspect module wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Check wiring and connectors for the cruise control module. On vehicles with a separate module, it may be located in the engine bay.

fixSteps.title: Replace module
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the cruise control module if it has failed. Some may require programming or calibration.

faq.q: Is cruise control a separate module?
faq.a: On many modern vehicles, cruise control is integrated into the ECM or a combined module. Older vehicles and those with adaptive cruise control may have a dedicated module.

faq.q: Can a faulty brake switch disable cruise control?
faq.a: Yes. A brake switch that appears to always be activated will prevent cruise control from engaging as a safety measure.

faq.q: Does this affect adaptive cruise control radar?
faq.a: If the vehicle has ACC, loss of communication with the cruise module will disable both conventional and adaptive cruise control functionality.

title: Lost Communication with Fuel Injector Control Module
shortDescription: A module has lost communication with the fuel injector control module.
fullDescription: Code U0105 indicates lost communication with the fuel injector control module. On some diesel vehicles and advanced direct-injection gasoline engines, a separate module controls the high-pressure fuel injectors. Loss of communication results in the engine being unable to inject fuel properly, causing no-start or severe running problems.
safetyImpact: Engine may not start or may run very poorly, creating a hazardous driving situation.

causes.cause: CAN bus wiring fault to fuel injector module

symptoms: Engine cranks but does not start

fixSteps.title: Verify module presence
fixSteps.instruction: Not all vehicles have a separate fuel injector module. Confirm your vehicle uses one. Scan to check if it responds.

fixSteps.title: Check power and ground
fixSteps.instruction: Verify the fuel injector module has proper power supply and ground connections.

fixSteps.title: Inspect CAN bus connection
fixSteps.instruction: Check CAN bus wiring from the fuel injector module to the main bus. Look for heat damage as this module is often in the engine compartment.

fixSteps.title: Replace module
fixSteps.instruction: If the module has failed, replace and program it. Clear codes and verify proper fuel injection operation.

faq.q: Do all vehicles have a separate fuel injector module?
faq.a: No. Many gasoline vehicles control injectors directly from the ECM. Diesel vehicles and some advanced direct-injection gasoline engines use a separate module for high-pressure injection control.

faq.q: Can engine heat damage this module?
faq.a: Yes. The fuel injector module is often located in the engine compartment and can be affected by extreme heat, which can damage electronics and connectors over time.

faq.q: Will the engine run without this module?
faq.a: No. If the fuel injector module controls the injectors, the engine cannot inject fuel without it and will not run.

title: Lost Communication with Glow Plug Control Module
shortDescription: A module has lost communication with the glow plug control module (diesel vehicles).
fullDescription: Code U0106 indicates loss of communication with the glow plug control module on diesel-powered vehicles. Glow plugs preheat the combustion chamber for cold starting. The module controls glow plug timing and duration. Loss of communication means glow plugs may not activate, making cold starting difficult or impossible.
safetyImpact: Diesel engine may not start in cold weather, potentially stranding the driver.

causes.cause: Connector corrosion at glow plug module
CAN bus wiring fault to module

symptoms: Glow plug indicator light not illuminating
Excessive white smoke on cold start

fixSteps.title: Check glow plug light
fixSteps.instruction: When turning the key to the ON position, the glow plug indicator should illuminate briefly. If it does not, the module may not be communicating.

fixSteps.title: Check fuse and power
fixSteps.instruction: Verify the glow plug module fuse (often high amperage, 60A+) and power supply to the module.

fixSteps.title: Inspect module connector
fixSteps.instruction: The glow plug module is usually located on top of the engine. Check for connector corrosion, heat damage, or loose connections.

fixSteps.title: Replace module
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the glow plug control module if faulty. Clear codes and verify glow plug operation on cold start.

faq.q: Will my diesel start without glow plugs?
faq.a: In warm weather, most diesel engines will start without glow plugs. In cold weather, starting becomes very difficult or impossible without preheat.

faq.q: How do glow plugs work?
faq.a: Glow plugs are electrically heated elements inside each cylinder that preheat the air in the combustion chamber to help ignite diesel fuel during cold starting.

faq.q: Can I test glow plugs individually?
faq.a: Yes. Each glow plug can be tested for resistance with a multimeter. A good glow plug typically reads 0.5-2 ohms. An open reading indicates a failed plug.

title: Lost Communication with Throttle Actuator Control Module
shortDescription: A module has lost communication with the electronic throttle control module.
fullDescription: Code U0107 indicates lost communication with the throttle actuator control module. On vehicles with electronic throttle control (drive-by-wire), this module or function controls the throttle plate position based on accelerator pedal input. Loss of communication can result in the engine entering reduced power mode or not responding to the accelerator.
safetyImpact: Engine may not respond to accelerator input or may enter reduced power mode, which can be dangerous in traffic.

causes.cause: CAN bus wiring fault to throttle module
Module connector corrosion from engine bay moisture

symptoms: Engine idles but does not respond to accelerator
Limp mode with limited throttle response

fixSteps.title: Scan for codes
fixSteps.instruction: Read all codes from the ECM and any throttle-related modules. Check for both U-codes and throttle-specific P-codes.

fixSteps.title: Inspect throttle body connections
fixSteps.instruction: Check the electrical connector at the throttle body for corrosion, damage, or loose fit. Clean with contact cleaner if needed.

fixSteps.title: Test throttle circuit
fixSteps.instruction: Verify power supply, ground, and CAN bus signals at the throttle body connector. Check accelerator pedal position sensor output.

fixSteps.title: Replace throttle body or module
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the throttle body assembly if the internal electronics have failed. Some vehicles require a throttle relearn procedure after replacement.

faq.q: What is drive-by-wire?
faq.a: Drive-by-wire replaces the mechanical throttle cable with an electronic system. The accelerator pedal sensor sends a signal to the ECM, which commands the throttle motor to open or close the throttle plate.

faq.q: Can I clean the throttle body to fix this?
faq.a: Cleaning the throttle body can fix throttle plate sticking issues but will not fix electronic communication faults. This code requires electrical diagnosis.

faq.q: What is reduced power mode?
faq.a: When the throttle system detects a fault, it limits throttle opening to protect the engine and occupants. The vehicle may only idle or have very limited acceleration.

title: Lost Communication with Alternative Fuel Control Module
shortDescription: A module has lost communication with the alternative fuel system control module.
fullDescription: Code U0108 indicates lost communication with the alternative fuel control module. This applies to vehicles equipped with alternative fuel systems such as CNG (compressed natural gas), LPG (liquefied petroleum gas), E85 flex-fuel, or hydrogen fuel systems. The module manages fuel system switching and alternative fuel injection parameters.
safetyImpact: Alternative fuel system may be disabled. Vehicle may default to conventional fuel operation if available.

causes.cause: CAN bus wiring fault to alternative fuel module
Aftermarket alternative fuel conversion module incompatibility

fixSteps.title: Scan codes
fixSteps.instruction: Read all codes. Identify if the alternative fuel module is on the network.

fixSteps.title: Check module connections
fixSteps.instruction: Locate the alternative fuel module and inspect its connector and wiring for damage.

fixSteps.title: Test power and communication
fixSteps.instruction: Verify the module has power, ground, and CAN bus connectivity.

fixSteps.title: Replace or reconfigure
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the module if faulty. For aftermarket conversions, verify compatibility with the vehicle's CAN bus protocol.

faq.q: Will my vehicle still run on gasoline?
faq.a: In most bi-fuel systems, the vehicle will default to gasoline if the alternative fuel system is unavailable. Dedicated alternative fuel vehicles may not start.

faq.q: Are aftermarket CNG/LPG conversions reliable?
faq.a: Quality varies significantly. Well-engineered conversions from reputable manufacturers with proper CAN bus integration are generally reliable. Budget conversions may have communication issues.

faq.q: Can I ignore this code if I only use gasoline?
faq.a: If you have a flex-fuel vehicle and only use gasoline, the code may not cause immediate problems. However, the check engine light will remain on, masking other potential issues.

title: Lost Communication with Fuel Pump Control Module
shortDescription: A module has lost communication with the fuel pump control module.
fullDescription: Code U0109 indicates lost communication with the fuel pump control module. Modern vehicles use a dedicated module to control fuel pump speed based on engine demand, improving fuel efficiency and reducing pump wear. Loss of communication can cause the fuel pump to run at full speed constantly or not run at all.
safetyImpact: Engine may stall or not start if the fuel pump cannot be controlled. Running at full speed constantly wastes fuel and can overheat the pump.

causes.cause: CAN bus wiring fault to pump module

symptoms: Engine cranks but does not start
Fuel pump runs continuously or not at all

fixSteps.title: Check fuel pump operation
fixSteps.instruction: Turn key to ON (do not start). Listen for fuel pump priming. If no pump sound, the module may not be commanding the pump.

fixSteps.title: Locate and inspect module
fixSteps.instruction: The fuel pump control module is often located under the vehicle near the fuel tank or under the rear seat. Inspect for damage and connector condition.

fixSteps.title: Test module circuits
fixSteps.instruction: Check power supply, ground, CAN bus signals, and fuel pump output at the module.

fixSteps.title: Replace module
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the fuel pump control module if faulty. Clear codes and verify the fuel pump operates correctly at various engine loads.

faq.q: Is the fuel pump module the same as the fuel pump?
faq.a: No. The fuel pump is inside the fuel tank. The fuel pump control module is an electronic unit that regulates the voltage/signal to the pump to control its speed.

faq.q: Can the module be bypassed?
faq.a: Bypassing the module to run the pump at full speed is possible as an emergency measure but wastes fuel and can overheat the pump. It is not a permanent solution.

faq.q: Where is the fuel pump module located?
faq.a: Typically under the vehicle near the fuel tank, on the frame rail, or sometimes under the rear seat. It is usually in a location exposed to road spray.

title: Lost Communication with Drive Motor Control Module
shortDescription: A module has lost communication with the electric drive motor control module (hybrid/EV).
fullDescription: Code U0110 indicates loss of communication with the drive motor control module on hybrid or electric vehicles. This module controls the electric motor that provides propulsion. Loss of communication can disable electric drive capability, potentially leaving the vehicle on engine power only (hybrids) or immobile (pure EVs).
safetyImpact: Electric drive capability is lost. Hybrids may operate on engine only with reduced performance. Pure EVs may not be drivable.

causes.cause: CAN bus wiring fault to motor controller

fixSteps.title: Scan all modules
fixSteps.instruction: Use a scanner capable of reading hybrid/EV systems. Check the motor control module and related high-voltage system codes.

fixSteps.title: Check high-voltage safety
fixSteps.instruction: Before working on any hybrid/EV high-voltage component, ensure the high-voltage system is properly de-energized following manufacturer procedures.

fixSteps.title: Inspect module connections
fixSteps.instruction: Check the motor controller connector for damage, corrosion, or vibration-related looseness. Also check the high-voltage interlock circuit.

fixSteps.title: Professional repair
fixSteps.instruction: Due to high-voltage safety risks, motor controller replacement should be performed by trained hybrid/EV technicians.

faq.q: Is it safe to work on the motor controller?
faq.a: No. The motor controller operates at high voltage (200-800V) which is lethal. Only trained and equipped technicians should work on high-voltage hybrid/EV components.

faq.q: Can my hybrid still drive on the engine alone?
faq.a: Most hybrids can operate on the engine alone in a degraded mode, but with reduced performance and no regenerative braking.

faq.q: How much does a motor controller cost?
faq.a: Motor controllers for hybrid/EV vehicles can range from $500 to $3000+ depending on the vehicle. Labor for high-voltage work adds significant cost.

title: Lost Communication with ABS Control Module
shortDescription: A module has lost communication with the Anti-lock Braking System control module.
fullDescription: Code U0121 indicates that one or more modules have lost communication with the ABS control module. The ABS module provides critical wheel speed data to other modules and controls ABS, traction control, and stability control. Loss of communication disables all these functions and may affect the speedometer on vehicles that derive speed from ABS data.
safetyImpact: ABS, traction control, and stability control are all non-functional. Speedometer may not work.

causes.cause: CAN bus wiring fault near ABS module
ABS module power or ground fault

symptoms: ABS, traction, and stability control lights on
Other modules report lost ABS communication
Cruise control may not function (no speed signal)

fixSteps.title: Scan all modules
fixSteps.instruction: Perform a full scan. Multiple modules reporting lost ABS communication confirms the ABS module is offline.

fixSteps.title: Check ABS module power
fixSteps.instruction: Verify battery voltage at the ABS module connector. Check main power fuse, relay, and ground connections.

fixSteps.title: Inspect CAN bus at ABS module
fixSteps.instruction: Check CAN bus voltage and continuity at the ABS module connector. Verify the module's CAN connection to the main bus.

fixSteps.title: Replace ABS module
fixSteps.instruction: If power, ground, and CAN bus are verified good, the ABS module has likely failed internally. Replace, program, and bleed brakes.

faq.q: Why does the speedometer stop working when ABS communication is lost?
faq.a: Many vehicles derive the vehicle speed signal from the ABS module's wheel speed data. If the ABS module is offline, the speed signal is lost.

faq.q: Can water damage the ABS module?
faq.a: Yes. The ABS module is often located in the engine bay where it can be exposed to water spray, especially on the driver-side fender. Water intrusion into the connector is a common failure mode.

faq.q: Is the ABS module expensive?
faq.a: New OEM ABS modules can cost $500-$2000+. Remanufactured units are often available at significant savings and are generally reliable with proper programming.

title: Lost Communication with Vehicle Dynamics Control Module
shortDescription: A module has lost communication with the vehicle dynamics control (stability) module.
fullDescription: Code U0122 indicates lost communication with the vehicle dynamics control module, which is the stability control system's primary controller. This module integrates data from wheel speed sensors, yaw rate sensor, steering angle sensor, and lateral accelerometer to maintain vehicle stability. Loss of communication disables all stability control functions.
safetyImpact: Vehicle stability control is completely disabled, removing one of the most important active safety systems.

causes.cause: CAN bus wiring fault to dynamics module

symptoms: ABS and traction control also disabled
U0122 in other modules (ECM, BCM, etc.)

fixSteps.title: Full vehicle scan
fixSteps.instruction: Scan all modules to confirm which ones are reporting lost communication with the dynamics module.

fixSteps.title: Check power and ground
fixSteps.instruction: Verify power supply and ground connections at the vehicle dynamics module. Check related fuses.

fixSteps.title: Test CAN bus at module
fixSteps.instruction: Verify CAN bus signals at the module connector. Check for proper voltage on CAN-H and CAN-L.

fixSteps.title: Replace module
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the vehicle dynamics module if it has failed. Program, calibrate sensors, and clear codes.

faq.q: Is the vehicle dynamics module separate from the ABS module?
faq.a: On some vehicles they are separate modules; on many modern vehicles, ABS and stability control are integrated into a single module.

faq.q: How important is stability control?
faq.a: Stability control is one of the most significant safety advances in automotive history, credited with reducing single-vehicle crashes by up to 50%. Repair should be prioritized.

faq.q: Can I disable stability control intentionally?
faq.a: Most vehicles have a button to temporarily disable stability control for specific situations (deep snow, track driving). It typically re-enables automatically at the next ignition cycle.

title: Lost Communication with Power Steering Control Module
shortDescription: A module has lost communication with the electric power steering (EPS) control module.
fullDescription: Code U0131 indicates lost communication with the power steering control module. Modern vehicles use electric power steering (EPS) that relies on electronic control. Loss of communication can result in the loss of power steering assist, requiring significantly more effort to steer, especially at low speeds.
safetyImpact: Power steering assist may be lost, making the vehicle very difficult to steer at low speeds and in parking situations.

causes.cause: CAN bus wiring fault to EPS module

symptoms: Steering may feel normal at highway speed but very heavy when parking

fixSteps.title: Scan codes
fixSteps.instruction: Read all codes. Check if the EPS module is responding to the scanner.

fixSteps.title: Check EPS fuse and power
fixSteps.instruction: The EPS system draws significant current. Check the EPS main fuse (often 60-80A) and verify power supply to the module.

fixSteps.title: Inspect EPS module connections
fixSteps.instruction: The EPS module is typically integrated with the steering column or rack. Check its connector and CAN bus wiring.

fixSteps.title: Replace EPS module
fixSteps.instruction: If the module has failed, replacement may involve the steering column or rack assembly depending on design. Programming and calibration are usually required.

faq.q: Can I still steer without power steering?
faq.a: Yes, but it requires much more physical effort, especially at low speeds and when parking. At highway speeds, the difference is less noticeable.

faq.q: Is electric power steering the same as hydraulic?
faq.a: No. EPS uses an electric motor to provide assist, while hydraulic systems use a pump and fluid. EPS is more efficient and allows for variable assist, but is entirely dependent on electronics.

faq.q: Is this a common failure?
faq.a: EPS module failures are not uncommon in vehicles with high mileage. Certain models are known for higher failure rates.

title: Lost Communication with Body Control Module
shortDescription: A module has lost communication with the Body Control Module (BCM).
fullDescription: Code U0140 indicates that one or more modules have lost communication with the Body Control Module (BCM). The BCM manages body electrical functions and often serves as a gateway module between different vehicle networks. Loss of BCM communication can affect numerous convenience features and may impact the gateway function between CAN buses.
safetyImpact: Body electrical functions including lighting may be affected. Gateway function loss can cascade to other systems.

causes.cause: CAN bus wiring fault to BCM
Water damage to BCM or connector

symptoms: Multiple body electrical features not working

fixSteps.title: Full system scan
fixSteps.instruction: Scan all modules. If many modules report lost BCM communication, the BCM itself is offline.

fixSteps.title: Check BCM power and ground
fixSteps.instruction: Locate the BCM (usually under the dashboard or in the kick panel). Verify power supply and ground connections.

fixSteps.title: Inspect for water damage
fixSteps.instruction: The BCM location can be susceptible to water leaks from the windshield, sunroof, or A/C drain. Inspect for moisture or corrosion.

fixSteps.title: Replace BCM
fixSteps.instruction: If the BCM has failed, replace and program it to the vehicle with all correct option configurations.

faq.q: Can a water leak cause BCM failure?
faq.a: Yes. Water leaks from the windshield, sunroof drains, or A/C condensation can reach the BCM and cause corrosion and eventual failure. This is one of the most common causes.

faq.q: What is the BCM gateway function?
faq.a: On many vehicles, the BCM acts as a gateway between different CAN bus networks, routing messages between the high-speed and medium-speed buses. If the BCM fails, inter-bus communication is lost.

faq.q: Do I need the dealer to program a new BCM?
faq.a: BCM programming typically requires a manufacturer-level or professional diagnostic tool with the vehicle's specific option data. Some aftermarket tools can also perform this function.

title: Lost Communication with Body Control Module A
shortDescription: A module has lost communication with Body Control Module A (on vehicles with multiple BCMs).
fullDescription: Code U0141 indicates lost communication with Body Control Module A. Some vehicles use multiple BCMs to distribute body electrical control. BCM A typically handles a specific set of functions. Loss of communication with this specific module affects only the functions it controls.
safetyImpact: Functions controlled by BCM A are disabled. Impact depends on which functions are assigned to this module.

causes.cause: CAN bus wiring fault to BCM A
BCM A power or ground issue
Water intrusion into BCM A location

symptoms: Subset of body electrical features not working
Specific lighting or convenience features disabled
Warning lights related to BCM A functions

fixSteps.title: Identify BCM A functions
fixSteps.instruction: Determine which functions BCM A controls on your specific vehicle. Scan all modules for related codes.

fixSteps.title: Check power and ground
fixSteps.instruction: Verify BCM A has proper power supply and ground connections at its location.

fixSteps.title: Inspect wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Check CAN bus wiring between BCM A and the main bus. Also inspect for environmental damage at the module location.

fixSteps.title: Replace if needed
fixSteps.instruction: Replace BCM A if it has failed. Program with correct configuration data.

faq.q: Why does my vehicle have multiple BCMs?
faq.a: Vehicles with many electronic features distribute control across multiple BCMs to manage the wiring complexity and processing load. Each BCM handles a subset of body functions.

faq.q: Can I use a BCM from a different vehicle?
faq.a: Generally no. BCMs are programmed with vehicle-specific options and VIN. A used BCM from the same model may work but will need reprogramming.

faq.q: How do I know which features BCM A controls?
faq.a: Your vehicle's service manual will detail which functions are assigned to each BCM. The features that stopped working when this code appeared are likely BCM A functions.

title: Lost Communication with Restraints Control Module
shortDescription: A module has lost communication with the airbag/restraints control module (ACM/RCM).
fullDescription: Code U0151 indicates lost communication with the Restraints Control Module (also called the Airbag Control Module or ACM). This is the central controller for all airbags, seat belt pretensioners, and occupant protection systems. Loss of communication means the entire SRS system is non-functional.
safetyImpact: All airbags and restraint systems are non-functional. No protection devices will deploy in a collision.

causes.cause: CAN bus wiring fault to ACM
Connector damage from under-seat storage or service

symptoms: Other modules report lost communication with restraints module

fixSteps.title: Verify communication loss
fixSteps.instruction: Attempt to communicate with the ACM using a scanner. If no response, the module is offline.

fixSteps.title: Check ACM power and ground
fixSteps.instruction: The ACM is typically under the center console. Verify power supply, ignition input, and ground connections.

fixSteps.title: Inspect CAN bus connection
fixSteps.instruction: Check CAN bus wires at the ACM connector. Also inspect for connector damage from items stored under seats.

fixSteps.title: Replace ACM
fixSteps.instruction: If the ACM has failed, replace and program it. All airbag circuits should be tested after module replacement.

faq.q: How serious is lost ACM communication?
faq.a: Very serious. Without the ACM communicating, no airbags will deploy in a crash. This should be treated as an urgent safety repair.

faq.q: Can moving items under the seat disconnect the ACM?
faq.a: Yes. On vehicles where the ACM is under the driver or passenger seat, large items stored under the seat can push against and disconnect the module's electrical connector.

faq.q: Will the vehicle pass inspection without ACM communication?
faq.a: No. An illuminated airbag warning light will fail a safety inspection in most jurisdictions.

title: Lost Communication with Instrument Cluster
shortDescription: A module has lost communication with the instrument cluster (dashboard display module).
fullDescription: Code U0155 indicates lost communication with the instrument cluster module. The instrument cluster displays critical vehicle information including speed, RPM, fuel level, temperature, and warning lights. It receives data from multiple modules over the CAN bus. Loss of communication results in a non-functional or partially functional dashboard.
safetyImpact: The driver cannot see critical vehicle information including speed, fuel level, and warning lights. This is a significant safety concern.

causes.cause: Instrument cluster power or ground fault
CAN bus wiring fault to instrument cluster

symptoms: Gauges all at zero or not moving

fixSteps.title: Check cluster power
fixSteps.instruction: Verify the instrument cluster fuse and check for power at the cluster connector behind the dashboard.

fixSteps.title: Inspect cluster connector
fixSteps.instruction: Remove the instrument cluster (usually held by a few screws) and inspect the electrical connector for corrosion or bent pins.

fixSteps.title: Test CAN bus at cluster
fixSteps.instruction: Check CAN bus voltage at the cluster connector. Verify the cluster has proper CAN-H and CAN-L signal.

fixSteps.title: Replace cluster
fixSteps.instruction: If the cluster has failed, replace it. The replacement may need to be programmed with the correct mileage and vehicle options.

faq.q: Can I drive without a working instrument cluster?
faq.a: Technically yes, but it is unsafe. Without a speedometer, fuel gauge, and warning lights, you cannot monitor critical vehicle information. Use a phone-based OBD app as a temporary alternative.

faq.q: Will a replacement cluster show the correct mileage?
faq.a: A replacement cluster must be programmed with the vehicle's correct mileage to comply with odometer tampering laws. Dealers and specialized shops can do this.

faq.q: Can a stepper motor failure look like a communication loss?
faq.a: No. Stepper motor failures cause individual gauge needles to not work but the cluster still communicates on the CAN bus. A communication loss means the entire cluster is offline.

title: Lost Communication with HVAC Control Module
shortDescription: A module has lost communication with the heating, ventilation, and air conditioning (HVAC) control module.
fullDescription: Code U0164 indicates lost communication with the HVAC control module. This module controls the climate control system including heating, air conditioning, blower speed, and air distribution. Loss of communication can result in the HVAC system defaulting to a fail-safe mode or becoming completely inoperative.
safetyImpact: Climate control is unavailable. Defogging capability may be lost, which is a safety concern in cold or wet conditions.

causes.cause: CAN bus wiring issue in dashboard area
Module LIN bus fault (if connected via LIN)

symptoms: Climate control panel blank or unresponsive

fixSteps.title: Scan for codes
fixSteps.instruction: Read all codes. Check if the HVAC module responds to the scanner. Note if other modules also have communication faults.

fixSteps.title: Check HVAC module fuse
fixSteps.instruction: Locate and check the HVAC control module fuse. Also verify the module has power by checking voltage at its connector.

fixSteps.title: Inspect module connector
fixSteps.instruction: Access the HVAC module behind the center dashboard panel. Check its connector for damage, corrosion, or loose pins.

fixSteps.title: Replace module
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the HVAC control module if it has failed. Some modules are integrated with the control panel and may require dashboard work.

faq.q: Why is the defroster not working?
faq.a: The defroster is controlled by the HVAC module. If the module has lost communication, it cannot activate the defroster, A/C compressor, or direct airflow to the windshield.

faq.q: Is the HVAC module part of the control panel?
faq.a: On some vehicles, the HVAC module is integrated into the climate control panel (the buttons/display). On others, it is a separate module behind the dashboard.

faq.q: Can I manually turn on the blower without the module?
faq.a: Not through normal controls. Some emergency bypasses exist but they provide full-speed blower only without temperature or mode control. This is not a recommended long-term solution.

title: Medium Speed CAN Communication Bus
shortDescription: Fault detected on the medium-speed CAN communication bus.
fullDescription:
Code U0014 indicates a fault on the medium-speed CAN bus, which typically operates at 125 kbps. This bus connects body control and comfort modules such as door modules, seat modules, mirror controls, and the instrument cluster on some vehicles.

The medium-speed CAN bus carries less time-critical data than the high-speed bus, primarily handling body electronics and comfort features. A fault on this bus can disable multiple convenience features simultaneously.

Diagnosis follows the same approach as high-speed CAN faults: check wiring, terminating resistors, and isolate individual modules by disconnecting them one at a time to find the module pulling down the bus.
safetyImpact: Convenience features may be disabled. Power windows, locks, and mirrors may become inoperative. Turn signals and lighting could also be affected on some vehicles.

causes.cause: Failed body control module pulling down the bus
Water intrusion into a door module connector
Failed terminating resistor on the medium-speed bus

symptoms: Power windows, locks, or mirrors not working

fixSteps.title: Full vehicle scan
fixSteps.instruction: Scan all modules. Note which modules on the medium-speed CAN bus have communication faults.

fixSteps.title: Measure CAN bus signals
fixSteps.instruction: Using an oscilloscope, measure the CAN-H and CAN-L signals on the medium-speed bus. CAN-H should swing between 2.5V and 3.5V, CAN-L between 1.5V and 2.5V.

fixSteps.title: Isolate faulty module
fixSteps.instruction: Disconnect modules on the medium-speed bus one at a time and recheck the bus signals. When removing a module restores normal signals, that module is the fault.

fixSteps.title: Repair or replace
fixSteps.instruction: Repair wiring damage or replace the faulty module. Recheck bus signals after repair.

faq.q: What is the difference between high-speed and medium-speed CAN?
faq.a: High-speed CAN runs at 500 kbps and connects powertrain modules. Medium-speed CAN runs at 125 kbps and connects body/comfort modules. They are separate networks.

faq.q: Can I still drive with this code?
faq.a: Usually yes, as the medium-speed bus handles comfort features, not powertrain. However, some safety features like turn signals may be affected, so verify those work before driving.

title: Medium Speed CAN Communication Bus (+)
shortDescription: Fault detected on the medium-speed CAN communication bus positive line.
fullDescription:
Code U0015 indicates a fault specifically on the CAN-High (positive) line of the medium-speed CAN bus. This is more specific than U0014, pinpointing the issue to the CAN-H wire.

The CAN-H line carries the dominant signal on the bus. If this line is shorted to ground, shorted to battery voltage, or open, the bus will fail to communicate. On some vehicles, the bus may still function in a degraded single-wire mode if only CAN-H is affected.

Diagnosis should focus on measuring the CAN-H line voltage and checking for shorts or opens along the wiring harness.
safetyImpact: Body electronics and comfort features may be disabled depending on which modules are on this bus.

causes.cause: CAN-H wire shorted to battery voltage
Connector pin damage on CAN-H circuit

fixSteps.title: Measure CAN-H voltage
fixSteps.instruction: With ignition on, measure CAN-H voltage at the diagnostic connector. It should oscillate between 2.5V and 3.5V. A steady voltage indicates a fault.

fixSteps.title: Check for shorts
fixSteps.instruction: With ignition off, measure resistance between CAN-H and ground. It should be above 10k ohms. Low resistance indicates a short.

fixSteps.title: Trace wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Follow the CAN-H wire through the harness checking for damage, especially at connectors and where the harness passes through body panels.

fixSteps.title: Repair wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Repair any damaged CAN-H wiring using proper automotive-grade solder and heat-shrink. Verify bus operation after repair.

faq.q: What voltage should CAN-H show?
faq.a: CAN-H should oscillate between approximately 2.5V (recessive) and 3.5V (dominant) when the bus is active. A steady voltage at 0V, 2.5V, or 5V indicates a problem.

faq.q: Can a bad module cause this?
faq.a: Yes, a module with a failed CAN transceiver can pull the CAN-H line to an incorrect voltage, causing bus-wide communication failures.

title: Medium Speed CAN Communication Bus (-)
shortDescription: Fault detected on the medium-speed CAN communication bus negative line.
fullDescription:
Code U0016 indicates a fault on the CAN-Low (negative) line of the medium-speed CAN bus. This complements U0015 by identifying the issue on the CAN-L wire specifically.

The CAN-L line works together with CAN-H to form a differential signaling pair. The bus reads the difference between the two lines. If CAN-L is shorted to power, shorted to ground, or open, the differential signal is corrupted and communication fails.

Some vehicles can operate in single-wire mode using only CAN-H if CAN-L fails, but this is not reliable and should be treated as a fault requiring repair.
safetyImpact: Body control features may be disabled. Lighting and signal functions could be affected.

causes.cause: CAN-L wire shorted to battery voltage
Failed CAN transceiver in a module pulling CAN-L

symptoms: U0014 and/or U0015 may also be present

fixSteps.title: Measure CAN-L voltage
fixSteps.instruction: With ignition on, measure CAN-L voltage. It should oscillate between 1.5V and 2.5V. A steady 0V or 5V indicates a fault.

fixSteps.title: Check for shorts
fixSteps.instruction: With ignition off, check CAN-L resistance to ground and to battery voltage. Both should be above 10k ohms.

fixSteps.title: Isolate the fault
fixSteps.instruction: Disconnect modules one at a time while monitoring CAN-L. When the fault clears, the last disconnected module or its wiring is the cause.

fixSteps.title: Repair
fixSteps.instruction: Repair the CAN-L wiring or replace the faulty module. Verify proper bus operation.

faq.q: Is CAN-L the same as CAN ground?
faq.a: No. CAN-L is a signal line that carries data. It is not a ground wire. CAN-L and CAN-H form a differential pair, and the data is read from the voltage difference between them.

faq.q: What happens if both CAN-H and CAN-L are damaged?
faq.a: Total bus failure. All modules on the medium-speed CAN bus will lose communication and set U-codes. Multiple body features will be completely disabled.

title: Low Speed CAN Communication Bus
shortDescription: Fault detected on the low-speed CAN communication bus.
fullDescription:
Code U0019 indicates a fault on the low-speed CAN bus, which typically operates at 33.3 kbps or similar low baud rates. This bus connects non-critical modules such as seat memory, mirror adjustment, and other comfort features.

The low-speed CAN bus is fault-tolerant by design. It can continue to operate in single-wire mode if one of its two wires fails. However, if both wires fail or the bus is severely compromised, all connected modules lose communication.

Diagnosis is similar to other CAN bus faults but the low-speed bus is often found in door harnesses and other areas prone to wire fatigue from repeated opening and closing.
safetyImpact: Primarily affects comfort features. Safety-critical systems are not typically on the low-speed CAN bus.

causes.cause: Wire breakage in door harness from repeated flexing
Water intrusion into a low-speed CAN module

fixSteps.title: Scan all modules
fixSteps.instruction: Perform a full scan. Note which comfort modules have lost communication.

fixSteps.title: Check door harness
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect wiring in door jamb areas where harnesses flex. Look for broken or stretched wires.

fixSteps.title: Test bus signals
fixSteps.instruction: Measure the low-speed CAN signals. The bus uses a different voltage scheme than high-speed CAN. Consult the service manual for your vehicle.

fixSteps.title: Repair wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Repair broken wires in the harness. Use flexible wire and proper connectors for door harness repairs.

faq.q: What is fault-tolerant CAN?
faq.a: Low-speed CAN is designed to continue operating if one wire fails. The bus can fall back to single-wire mode, maintaining communication at reduced reliability. This is why low-speed CAN is used for non-critical systems.

faq.q: Why do door modules commonly fail?
faq.a: The wiring harness between the door and body flexes every time the door opens and closes. Over years, individual wires can break inside the harness, often intermittently at first.

title: Vehicle Communication Bus A Performance
shortDescription: Communication bus A is not performing within expected parameters.
fullDescription:
Code U0028 indicates that while the communication bus is not completely failed, it is experiencing performance issues such as excessive error frames, slow response times, or intermittent signal degradation.

This code often appears before a complete bus failure. It can indicate a developing wiring problem, a module with a marginal CAN transceiver, or electromagnetic interference affecting the bus signals.

Because this is a performance code rather than a total failure code, the vehicle may still operate with intermittent symptoms. However, the underlying cause should be addressed before it progresses to a complete bus failure.
safetyImpact: Intermittent communication may cause unpredictable behavior of vehicle systems. Could affect safety features sporadically.

causes.cause: Marginal CAN bus wiring connection (high resistance)
CAN bus wire partially damaged but not fully broken

symptoms: Symptoms that come and go, especially with vibration

fixSteps.title: Check for aftermarket devices
fixSteps.instruction: Identify any aftermarket devices connected to the OBD-II port or CAN bus (alarms, trackers, performance tuners). Disconnect them and retest.

fixSteps.title: Inspect connections
fixSteps.instruction: Check all CAN bus connectors for corrosion, loose pins, or damage. Pay special attention to areas exposed to moisture.

fixSteps.title: Monitor bus quality
fixSteps.instruction: Use an oscilloscope to monitor bus signal quality. Look for noise, ringing, or abnormal waveforms that indicate a developing fault.

fixSteps.title: Repair or replace
fixSteps.instruction: Address any wiring or connector issues found. Replace modules with degrading transceivers.

faq.q: Can an OBD-II plug-in device cause this?
faq.a: Yes. Cheap OBD-II Bluetooth adapters, GPS trackers, and insurance dongles can introduce noise onto the CAN bus, causing performance issues. Try removing any such devices.

faq.q: Is this code serious?
faq.a: It is a warning that the bus is degrading. While not an immediate failure, it should be diagnosed before it becomes a complete communication failure.

title: Vehicle Communication Bus C Off
shortDescription: Vehicle communication bus C is not active when it should be.
fullDescription:
Code U0033 indicates that communication bus C is not operational. Bus C is typically a tertiary communication bus used for infotainment, diagnostic, or other secondary functions depending on the vehicle manufacturer.

On many modern vehicles, bus C is used for the multimedia/infotainment system, connecting the head unit, amplifier, CD changer, navigation module, and rear-seat entertainment. Loss of this bus disables these features but typically does not affect vehicle drivability.

Diagnosis involves verifying the bus wiring and determining which module is responsible for keeping the bus active (the gateway module or head unit in most cases).
safetyImpact: Infotainment and multimedia features may be disabled. Backup camera may not function if it uses this bus.

causes.cause: Head unit or infotainment module not powering up
Gateway module not routing to bus C

fixSteps.title: Check fuses
fixSteps.instruction: Check all fuses related to the radio, infotainment, and multimedia system. Replace any blown fuses.

fixSteps.title: Verify head unit power
fixSteps.instruction: Check that the head unit is receiving power and ground. Verify both constant battery and ignition-switched power.

fixSteps.title: Check bus C wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect the communication bus wiring to the head unit and other infotainment modules.

fixSteps.title: Replace module
fixSteps.instruction: If the head unit or gateway module is not initializing bus C, replace the faulty module.

faq.q: Will this code affect driving?
faq.a: Generally no. Bus C typically handles infotainment features. The vehicle should be safe to drive, but you may lose radio, navigation, and backup camera functions.

faq.q: Can a dead radio cause this?
faq.a: Yes. If the radio/head unit is the master device on bus C and it fails, the entire bus will be inactive, triggering this code.

title: Vehicle Communication Bus A Data Rate Performance
shortDescription: Data rate on communication bus A is not within expected parameters.
fullDescription:
Code U0041 indicates that the data rate on communication bus A is abnormal. The bus is active but data is being transmitted at incorrect speeds or with timing errors, causing intermittent communication problems.

This can be caused by a module transmitting at the wrong baud rate (possibly due to incorrect firmware or a hardware fault in its CAN controller), or by wiring issues that degrade signal quality enough to cause timing errors.

This code may appear after a module has been replaced with an incorrect part number or after a failed firmware update that has changed a module's communication settings.
safetyImpact: Intermittent communication errors may cause unpredictable behavior. Safety systems could be affected if their data is delayed or corrupted.

causes.cause: Module with incorrect firmware or part number installed
Failed module firmware update leaving incorrect baud rate
CAN bus wiring issue causing signal reflections

symptoms: Modules occasionally dropping off the network
Warning lights that appear and disappear

fixSteps.title: Check recent work
fixSteps.instruction: Determine if any modules were recently replaced or reprogrammed. Verify correct part numbers for all recently installed modules.

fixSteps.title: Verify module firmware
fixSteps.instruction: Check firmware versions of all modules on the bus. Update any modules with outdated or incorrect firmware.

fixSteps.title: Check terminating resistors
fixSteps.instruction: Measure the overall bus resistance. It should be approximately 60 ohms (two 120-ohm resistors in parallel).
