A known issue in some Hyundai/Kia models involves the alternator causing CAN bus noise, which triggers U1900. This is particularly common in certain model years of the Sonata, Elantra, and Optima.
safetyImpact: Depends on the affected modules. May range from minor convenience feature loss to significant safety system impairment.

causes.cause: Alternator generating CAN bus electrical noise
Failed module on the CAN bus

fixSteps.title: Check battery and alternator
fixSteps.instruction: Test the battery and alternator output. Check for excessive AC ripple from the alternator, which is a known cause of U1900 in Hyundai/Kia vehicles.

fixSteps.title: Clear and retest
fixSteps.instruction: Clear the code and drive the vehicle. If it returns immediately, there is an active fault. If it stays cleared, it may have been a one-time event.

fixSteps.title: Full vehicle scan
fixSteps.instruction: Scan all modules. Identify all modules with U1900 and any companion codes that may indicate the specific cause.

fixSteps.title: Diagnose CAN bus
fixSteps.instruction: If the code persists, check CAN bus wiring, ground connections, and isolate the fault by disconnecting modules.

faq.q: Can a bad alternator cause U1900?
faq.a: Yes. This is a well-known issue in Hyundai/Kia vehicles. A failing alternator can produce electrical noise (AC ripple) that interferes with the CAN bus signals, triggering U1900.

faq.q: Why do I have U1900 in many modules?
faq.a: Like Nissan's U1000, Hyundai/Kia's U1900 is a general CAN bus fault flag. Every module that detects the bus problem stores its own U1900. There is usually one root cause affecting them all.

title: SCP Communication Bus Fault (Ford)
shortDescription: Standard Corporate Protocol (SCP) communication bus fault in Ford vehicles.
fullDescription:
Code U1113 is a Ford manufacturer-specific code indicating a fault on the Standard Corporate Protocol (SCP) communication bus. SCP is Ford's legacy communication protocol used in vehicles from the mid-1990s through the mid-2000s.

The SCP bus operates similarly to CAN but uses a different protocol. Faults on this bus prevent modules from sharing data, causing multiple system failures. Common areas of failure include the splice packs where multiple module connections join the main bus harness.

This code has been superseded by CAN bus codes in newer Ford vehicles, but is still commonly seen in older F-150s, Explorers, Expeditions, and Crown Victorias.
safetyImpact: Multiple vehicle systems may lose communication. Impact depends on which modules use the SCP bus in the specific vehicle.

causes.cause: SCP bus wire short or open
Failed module pulling down the SCP bus

symptoms: Scanner communication issues with certain modules

fixSteps.title: Scan for codes
fixSteps.instruction: Use a Ford-compatible scanner to read codes from all modules. Note which modules have SCP communication faults.

fixSteps.title: Check SCP bus voltage
fixSteps.instruction: Measure the SCP bus signal at the OBD-II connector. The signal should show proper voltage swings during communication.

fixSteps.title: Inspect splice packs
fixSteps.instruction: Ford SCP systems use splice packs (junction connectors) where modules connect to the bus. These are common failure points. Locate and inspect them.

fixSteps.title: Repair or replace
fixSteps.instruction: Repair corroded splices or wiring. Replace any failed module identified during isolation testing.

faq.q: What is SCP?
faq.a: Standard Corporate Protocol is Ford's proprietary communication bus used before CAN became standard. It connects modules on a two-wire bus similar in concept to CAN but with different signal characteristics.

faq.q: Is my vehicle too old for CAN?
faq.a: If your Ford was built before approximately 2005, it likely uses SCP or a mix of SCP and CAN. Vehicles from 2008 onward are all CAN. Check your service manual to determine which bus your vehicle uses.

title: CAN Timeout - No Message from ECM (Volkswagen/Audi)
shortDescription: CAN bus timeout error indicating no message received from the ECM in VW/Audi vehicles.
fullDescription:
Code U1600 is a Volkswagen/Audi manufacturer-specific code indicating that a module has timed out waiting for a CAN message from the engine control module. This is the VW/Audi equivalent of the generic U0100 code.

In the VW/Audi system, each module expects to receive periodic messages from the ECM. If the ECM does not send a message within the expected timeframe, the receiving module sets U1600.

Common in VW/Audi vehicles after battery disconnect, ECM failure, or CAN bus wiring faults. The Gateway module (J533) in VW/Audi vehicles is a critical component that routes messages between different CAN buses, and a gateway fault can also trigger this code.
safetyImpact: ECM communication lost. Engine management data not available to other modules. May affect transmission, ABS, and other systems.

causes.cause: CAN bus wiring fault between ECM and gateway

fixSteps.title: Check battery
fixSteps.instruction: VW/Audi vehicles are very sensitive to battery voltage. Test and ensure battery is above 12.4V.

fixSteps.title: Scan with VCDS
fixSteps.instruction: Use VCDS (VAG-COM) or a VW/Audi compatible scanner to perform a full auto-scan. Note which modules have communication faults.

fixSteps.title: Check gateway module
fixSteps.instruction: The J533 gateway module is usually located behind the dashboard or under the driver's footwell. Check its connections and power.

fixSteps.title: Diagnose ECM
fixSteps.instruction: If the ECM is not communicating, check its power supply, ground connections, and CAN bus wiring.

faq.q: What is VCDS?
faq.a: VCDS (VAG-COM Diagnostic System) is a third-party scan tool specifically designed for VW/Audi/Skoda/SEAT vehicles. It provides much deeper diagnostic access than generic OBD-II scanners and is widely used by VW/Audi enthusiasts and independent shops.

faq.q: Is the gateway module important?
faq.a: Yes, the gateway module (J533) in VW/Audi vehicles is critically important. It connects all the CAN buses together. A failed gateway can cause widespread communication failures across the entire vehicle.

title: CAN-C Bus Off (Chrysler/Dodge/Jeep)
shortDescription: Interior CAN-C communication bus is offline in Chrysler/Dodge/Jeep vehicles.
fullDescription:
Code U1751 is a Chrysler/Dodge/Jeep (Stellantis) manufacturer-specific code indicating that the CAN-C (interior) communication bus has gone offline. CAN-C is the bus used for body and interior modules in FCA vehicles.

CAN-C connects modules such as the instrument cluster, BCM, radio, HVAC, door modules, and other interior electronics. When CAN-C goes offline, these interior features lose communication.

This is distinct from the CAN-B (diagnostic) bus and the CAN-IHS (powertrain) bus. The specific bus designation helps narrow down which wiring harness and which modules to investigate.
safetyImpact: Interior electronics and body features disabled. Turn signals and interior lighting may be affected.

causes.cause: CAN-C bus wiring short or open in dashboard area
Failed interior module pulling down CAN-C
Aftermarket device on CAN-C causing bus overload

symptoms: Instrument cluster blank or partially working

fixSteps.title: Scan all modules
fixSteps.instruction: Use a Chrysler-compatible scanner (wiTECH or equivalent) to perform a full bus scan. Note which CAN-C modules respond and which do not.

fixSteps.title: Check for aftermarket devices
fixSteps.instruction: Remove any aftermarket devices connected to the interior wiring (remote starters, alarm systems, OBD-II plug-ins).

fixSteps.title: Measure CAN-C signals
fixSteps.instruction: Check the CAN-C bus voltage and signal quality at the OBD-II connector. Verify proper differential signaling.

fixSteps.title: Isolate fault
fixSteps.instruction: Disconnect CAN-C modules one at a time to identify the module or wiring section causing the bus to go offline.

faq.q: What is CAN-C?
faq.a: In Chrysler/Dodge/Jeep vehicles, the CAN bus is split into multiple networks: CAN-C for interior/body modules, CAN-B for diagnostics, and CAN-IHS for powertrain. CAN-C handles comfort and convenience features.

faq.q: Can a remote starter cause this?
faq.a: Yes. Aftermarket remote start systems often tap into the CAN-C bus for body control functions. A poorly installed or failing remote starter can short or overload the CAN-C bus.

title: Implausible Engine Torque Signal Received (BMW)
shortDescription: BMW control module received an implausible engine torque data signal over the CAN bus.
fullDescription:
Code U1424 is a BMW manufacturer-specific code indicating that a module (typically the transmission control module or stability control module) has received an implausible engine torque signal from the DME (Digital Motor Electronics, BMW's term for ECM).

Engine torque data is critical for smooth transmission shifts and proper stability control intervention. When the torque signal is implausible (does not match expected values given current engine speed, throttle position, and operating conditions), receiving modules cannot perform their functions correctly.

This code can be caused by a DME internal fault, a CAN bus data integrity issue, or a sensor problem that causes the DME to calculate an incorrect torque value.
safetyImpact: Transmission shift quality degraded. Stability control (DSC) may not function correctly. Engine performance may be affected.

causes.cause: DME software fault or outdated calibration
Sensor input error causing incorrect torque calculation

fixSteps.title: Scan with BMW diagnostics
fixSteps.instruction: Use ISTA or a BMW-compatible scanner to read fault memory from the DME and transmission modules. Check for related sensor codes.

fixSteps.title: Check DME software version
fixSteps.instruction: Verify the DME software is up to date. BMW frequently releases software updates that correct torque calculation algorithms.

fixSteps.title: Check engine sensors
fixSteps.instruction: Verify that MAF, throttle position, and crankshaft position sensors are reading correctly. Bad sensor input causes incorrect torque calculations.

fixSteps.title: Update DME software
fixSteps.instruction: Flash the DME with the latest software version. This often corrects torque signal calculation issues.

faq.q: What is DME?
faq.a: DME stands for Digital Motor Electronics. It is BMW's name for the engine control module (ECM). It manages all engine functions including fuel injection, ignition timing, and torque output calculation.

faq.q: Is this a transmission problem?
faq.a: Usually no. The problem is typically in the torque data being sent by the DME, not in the transmission itself. The transmission is reacting correctly to incorrect information.

title: Vehicle Communication Bus E Off
shortDescription: Vehicle communication bus E is not active when expected.
fullDescription:
Code U0077 indicates that communication bus E is not operational. Bus E is typically a secondary or auxiliary communication bus used for specific subsystems such as entertainment, telematics, or advanced driver assistance systems depending on the vehicle manufacturer.

This bus may use CAN, LIN, MOST (Media Oriented Systems Transport), or Ethernet depending on the vehicle. Loss of bus E communication disables whatever subsystem it supports.

Modern luxury vehicles may have five or more separate communication buses, with Bus E often handling the latest technology features like 360-degree camera systems, heads-up display, or wireless phone charging modules.
safetyImpact: Impact depends on what systems use bus E. Typically affects advanced features and infotainment rather than safety-critical systems.

causes.cause: Bus E master module not powering up
Blown fuse for bus E subsystem
Gateway not routing to bus E

fixSteps.title: Check fuses
fixSteps.instruction: Check all fuses related to the subsystem on bus E. Replace any blown fuses.

fixSteps.title: Identify bus E modules
fixSteps.instruction: Using the wiring diagram, identify which modules are on bus E and which is the master/initiator.

fixSteps.title: Check master module
fixSteps.instruction: Verify the master module for bus E has power, ground, and its connector is properly seated.

fixSteps.title: Diagnose bus E
fixSteps.instruction: Check the bus E wiring and protocol. Some bus E implementations use fiber optic (MOST) which requires different diagnostic techniques.

faq.q: What technology does bus E typically handle?
faq.a: Bus E varies by manufacturer but often handles advanced technology features like surround-view cameras, heads-up display, advanced infotainment, or telematics. It is usually the newest bus added to the vehicle platform.

faq.q: Is bus E always CAN?
faq.a: No. Depending on the vehicle, bus E could be CAN, LIN, MOST (fiber optic for multimedia), or automotive Ethernet. The diagnostic approach depends on the bus type.

title: Vehicle Communication Bus D Performance
shortDescription: Communication bus D is experiencing performance degradation.
fullDescription:
Code U0056 indicates that communication bus D is active but performing below expected standards. The bus is communicating but with errors, delays, or data integrity issues that affect reliability.

Bus D is typically a secondary communication network handling body electronics, comfort features, or diagnostic communication depending on the vehicle manufacturer and architecture.

Performance degradation can manifest as intermittent feature operation, slow response to controls, or partial functionality. The bus is not completely failed but is not reliable. This often precedes a complete bus failure if the underlying cause is not addressed.
safetyImpact: Secondary systems may operate intermittently. Typically does not affect safety-critical systems.

causes.cause: Wire insulation damage allowing intermittent shorts

symptoms: Occasional warning lights that clear themselves
Features work sometimes but not always

fixSteps.title: Identify affected systems
fixSteps.instruction: Note which features are intermittent. This helps identify which modules on bus D are affected.

fixSteps.title: Check connectors
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect all connectors on bus D for corrosion, loose pins, or water damage. Clean and reseat connections.

fixSteps.title: Monitor bus signals
fixSteps.instruction: Use an oscilloscope to monitor bus D signal quality over time. Look for noise, signal degradation, or error frames.

fixSteps.title: Repair connections
fixSteps.instruction: Repair or replace corroded connectors and damaged wiring. Verify bus performance after repair.

faq.q: Why do features work sometimes but not others?
faq.a: A performance degradation code means the bus is partially functional. Messages may get through sometimes but not reliably. Temperature changes, vibration, or moisture can cause a marginal connection to intermittently fail.

faq.q: Will this get worse?
faq.a: Usually yes. Performance degradation often indicates a developing fault (corroding connector, fraying wire) that will eventually cause a complete bus failure. Repair it before it fails completely.

title: Invalid Data Received from BCM - Door/Liftgate Status
shortDescription: Invalid door or liftgate status data received from the body control module.
fullDescription:
Code U0422 indicates that a module has received invalid door or liftgate status data from the body control module (BCM). The BCM monitors door ajar switches and sends this information to other modules including the instrument cluster, interior lighting, and security system.

Invalid door status data can cause the instrument cluster to show wrong doors as open, interior lights to not work correctly, or the vehicle security system to not arm properly.

The most common cause is a faulty door ajar switch or its wiring. These switches are located in the door latches and are exposed to weather, dirt, and wear from daily use.
safetyImpact: Door ajar warning may not display correctly. Interior lights may not respond to doors. Security system may not arm properly.

causes.cause: Faulty door ajar switch in door latch
BCM software fault processing door inputs

symptoms: Incorrect door ajar display on instrument cluster
Interior lights stay on or do not come on
Door chime when all doors are closed

fixSteps.title: Check door ajar switches
fixSteps.instruction: Open and close each door while watching the door ajar display. Identify which door's switch is not responding correctly.

fixSteps.title: Inspect door latch
fixSteps.instruction: Check the door latch mechanism where the ajar switch is located. Spray with penetrating lubricant if the switch is sticking.

fixSteps.title: Test switch wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Check the wiring from the door ajar switch to the BCM for continuity and proper grounding.

fixSteps.title: Replace switch or latch
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the faulty door ajar switch. On many vehicles, this requires replacing the entire door latch assembly.

faq.q: Why does the interior light stay on?
faq.a: The BCM turns on interior lights when it detects a door open. If a door ajar switch is stuck in the open position, the BCM thinks the door is always open and keeps the lights on.

faq.q: Can WD-40 fix a stuck door switch?
faq.a: A penetrating lubricant can sometimes free a stuck door ajar switch temporarily. However, if the switch is worn out, it will stick again and should be replaced for a permanent fix.

title: Invalid Data Received from Turbocharger/Supercharger Boost Control Module
shortDescription: Invalid data received from the forced induction boost control module.
fullDescription:
Code U0256 indicates that a module has received invalid data from the turbocharger or supercharger boost control module. On vehicles with electronically controlled boost (variable geometry turbo, electronic wastegate, or supercharger bypass valve), a dedicated module or subsystem manages boost pressure.

Invalid boost data can cause the ECM to miscalculate fueling and timing, potentially leading to engine damage from overboosting. The ECM may enter a protective mode, significantly reducing power.

This code is common in vehicles with twin-turbo systems, variable geometry turbos, or electrically assisted turbochargers where the boost control is managed by a separate module rather than directly by the ECM.
safetyImpact: Overboost condition possible if data is invalid in a way that removes boost limits. Reduced power mode may engage, limiting acceleration capability.

causes.cause: Boost pressure sensor sending bad data to module

fixSteps.title: Scan for codes
fixSteps.instruction: Read codes from the ECM and boost control module. Check for additional boost pressure sensor and turbo codes.

fixSteps.title: Check boost pressure sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Verify the boost pressure sensor reading matches actual pressure using a mechanical gauge. Replace if inaccurate.

fixSteps.title: Inspect boost actuator
fixSteps.instruction: Check the electronic wastegate or VGT actuator for proper operation. Verify it responds to commands via scan tool.

fixSteps.title: Update or replace module
fixSteps.instruction: Update the boost control module software or replace if hardware has failed.

faq.q: Can this damage my engine?
faq.a: If the boost control data is invalid in a way that allows excessive boost, yes. However, the ECM typically has backup boost limits. Reduced power mode is the ECM protecting the engine.

faq.q: Why did I lose power?
faq.a: The ECM enters a protective reduced power mode when boost data is invalid. This limits boost pressure to prevent potential engine damage from uncontrolled boost.

title: Invalid Data Received From Communication Module
shortDescription: The receiving module has detected invalid data from the communication/telematics module.
fullDescription:
U0500 indicates that data received from the communication control module (OnStar, SYNC, telematics, or similar) is out of expected range or missing required signals. The module is present on the bus but the data payload is malformed.

This typically points to an internal fault in the telematics module or a software mismatch after a module replacement.
safetyImpact: Emergency call (eCall) features, stolen vehicle tracking, and remote services may be disabled.

causes.cause: Software version mismatch after module replacement

fixSteps.title: Retrieve all module codes
fixSteps.instruction: Scan all modules to identify which module reports U0500 and which communication module is the source.

fixSteps.title: Update software
fixSteps.instruction: Check for manufacturer software updates for the telematics module and perform reflash if available.

fixSteps.title: Replace telematics module
fixSteps.instruction: If software is current and wiring is good, replace the telematics module and program it to the vehicle.

faq.q: Will this affect driving?
faq.a: Usually no — this is a comfort/safety feature module. The engine and brakes continue to function normally.

title: Invalid Data Received From Communication Module B
shortDescription: Secondary communication module is providing invalid data on the network.
fullDescription:
U0501 is similar to U0500 but points to a secondary communication module (e.g., satellite radio module, Wi-Fi hotspot module). Data is being transmitted but is out of range or corrupt.

Often appears alongside U0500 when the primary telematics unit has internal faults affecting multiple functional areas.
safetyImpact: Infotainment features may be limited; no direct safety impact.

fixSteps.title: Identify module
fixSteps.instruction: Use a factory scan tool to determine which specific sub-module is flagged.

fixSteps.title: Power cycle
fixSteps.instruction: Disconnect battery for 10 minutes and reconnect to reset the module.

fixSteps.title: Reprogram or replace
fixSteps.instruction: If code returns, reprogram the module; replace if unresponsive.

faq.q: Can I ignore this code?
faq.a: Yes, it only affects infotainment features. The vehicle is safe to drive.

title: Invalid Data Received From Vehicle Immobilizer Control Module
shortDescription: The immobilizer module is transmitting invalid data, preventing normal engine start.
fullDescription:
U0510 indicates the receiving module (usually ECM or BCM) is getting bad data from the immobilizer/anti-theft module. The module is communicating but the key authentication data is invalid.

This often causes a no-start condition because the ECM requires a valid immobilizer signal to enable fuel and ignition.
safetyImpact: Vehicle may not start. No direct driving safety impact, but stranding is possible.

fixSteps.title: Test with spare key
fixSteps.instruction: Try starting the vehicle with a different programmed key to rule out key fault.

fixSteps.title: Inspect antenna ring
fixSteps.instruction: Check the transponder antenna around the ignition lock for damage or loose connector.

fixSteps.title: Reprogram immobilizer
fixSteps.instruction: Perform immobilizer relearn procedure with a capable scan tool.

faq.q: Can I bypass the immobilizer?
faq.a: No — the immobilizer is integral to theft prevention and is required for engine start by design.

title: Invalid Data Received From Headlamp Leveling Module
shortDescription: The headlamp leveling module is sending invalid data on the network.
fullDescription:
U0515 is set when the BCM or lighting module detects invalid data from the auto-leveling headlamp control module. Self-leveling headlamps adjust aim based on vehicle load and ride height.

The module may be present but its height sensor data or actuator feedback is out of range.
safetyImpact: Headlamp aim may be incorrect, potentially dazzling oncoming drivers or poor road illumination.

fixSteps.title: Check height sensors
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect front and rear ride height sensors for physical damage or broken linkages.

fixSteps.title: Scan live data
fixSteps.instruction: Monitor sensor inputs while rocking the vehicle to confirm sensor response.

fixSteps.title: Replace faulty part
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the sensor or module as indicated by diagnosis.

faq.q: Will my headlights still work?
faq.a: Yes, but they may be stuck in a single position. Manual aim adjustment may be needed temporarily.

title: Invalid Data Received From Power Steering Control Module
shortDescription: The power steering control module is transmitting invalid data on the network.
fullDescription:
U0521 is set when another module (typically BCM or ECM) detects invalid data from the electric power steering (EPS) module. The EPS module may be transmitting but data such as steering torque or assist level is out of range.

EPS may drop to manual (no-assist) mode as a safety precaution.
safetyImpact: Loss of power steering assist increases steering effort significantly, especially at low speeds.

fixSteps.title: Check battery health
fixSteps.instruction: EPS systems are voltage-sensitive. Load test the battery and verify charging system output.

fixSteps.title: Inspect EPS connector
fixSteps.instruction: Locate the EPS module (often on the steering column or rack) and inspect its connector for corrosion.

fixSteps.title: Replace EPS module
fixSteps.instruction: If diagnostics confirm module failure, replace and perform steering angle relearn.

faq.q: Is it safe to drive without power steering?
faq.a: The vehicle is drivable but steering effort is much higher, especially parking. Fix promptly.

title: Invalid Data Received From Suspension Control Module
shortDescription: The suspension control module is transmitting invalid data.
fullDescription:
U0530 is set when data from the suspension control module (air suspension, magnetic ride, adaptive dampers) is invalid. The module is online but reporting out-of-range values.

Suspension usually enters a default/fail-safe mode with fixed damping.
safetyImpact: Ride quality and handling may be degraded; vehicle height may be incorrect.

causes.cause: Faulty ride height or damper sensor

fixSteps.title: Retrieve suspension codes
fixSteps.instruction: Read codes directly from the suspension module for more specific sub-codes.

fixSteps.title: Inspect sensors
fixSteps.instruction: Check all four ride height sensors and damper wiring for damage.

fixSteps.title: Replace module or sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the faulty component based on diagnosis.

faq.q: Is the vehicle safe to drive?
faq.a: Yes for short distances at moderate speed, but handling may be compromised. Address soon.

title: Invalid Data Received From Adaptive Front Lighting Module
shortDescription: Adaptive front lighting (AFS) module is providing invalid data.
fullDescription: U0600 is set when the receiving module detects invalid data from the AFS module, which swivels headlamps in response to steering input. The module may be communicating with incorrect steering angle or speed data.
safetyImpact: Adaptive lighting disabled; standard headlight function typically retained.

fixSteps.title: Steering angle relearn
fixSteps.instruction: Perform steering angle sensor calibration with scan tool.

fixSteps.title: Inspect swivel motors
fixSteps.instruction: Check headlamp swivel motors for mechanical binding.

fixSteps.title: Replace AFS module
fixSteps.instruction: Replace module and reprogram if other steps fail.

faq.q: Can I drive with AFS disabled?
faq.a: Yes, standard low/high beams continue to function normally.

title: Invalid Data Received From Immobilizer
shortDescription: Immobilizer module is providing invalid authentication data.
fullDescription: U0615 is a variant of the immobilizer communication fault where the authentication handshake between the ECM and the immobilizer produces invalid data. Engine may run briefly then stall, or fail to start.
safetyImpact: No-start or stall condition possible.

fixSteps.title: Try spare key
fixSteps.instruction: Test with a second programmed key.

fixSteps.title: Relearn keys
fixSteps.instruction: Perform full key relearn procedure per service information.

fixSteps.title: Replace module
fixSteps.instruction: Replace immobilizer module and program.

faq.q: Why does my car start then stall?
faq.a: The immobilizer allows a brief start then cuts fuel when authentication fails.

title: Invalid Data Received From Radio
shortDescription: The radio/infotainment head unit is sending invalid data on the network.
fullDescription: U0625 is set when modules detect invalid data from the radio/head unit. The head unit transmits audio settings, climate info and VIN to other modules; invalid data can affect various features.
safetyImpact: Infotainment features affected; no drivability impact.

fixSteps.title: Power cycle
fixSteps.instruction: Disconnect battery 10 minutes to reset head unit.

fixSteps.title: Check aftermarket install
fixSteps.instruction: If aftermarket radio is installed, verify CAN interface wiring.

fixSteps.title: Replace or reprogram head unit
fixSteps.instruction: Reflash software or replace head unit if faulty.

faq.q: Can this be from my aftermarket stereo?
faq.a: Yes — missing CAN bus signals from a non-OEM radio commonly cause this code.

title: Invalid Data Received - Data Bus
shortDescription: General invalid data fault on the vehicle data bus.
fullDescription: U0640 is a broad invalid-data fault indicating the receiving module cannot validate data from one or more modules on the bus. Often stored alongside more specific U-codes.
safetyImpact: Variable depending on which data is invalid.

fixSteps.title: Check battery
fixSteps.instruction: Low battery voltage can cause bus corruption; load test first.

fixSteps.title: Inspect bus wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Check CAN-H/CAN-L wiring for damage.

fixSteps.title: Isolate faulty module
fixSteps.instruction: Use module-by-module disconnect to isolate the offender.

faq.q: Is this one code or many?
faq.a: It's often a symptom of broader bus faults — check all modules.

title: Invalid Data Received From Convertible Roof Module
shortDescription: The convertible/retractable roof module is reporting invalid data.
fullDescription: U0650 indicates the BCM or roof controller detects invalid data about the convertible top position or actuator feedback. The roof may refuse to operate until resolved.
safetyImpact: Top may not operate — vehicle can still be driven.

fixSteps.title: Scan for sub-codes
fixSteps.instruction: Read roof module for specific sensor faults.

fixSteps.title: Inspect position switches
fixSteps.instruction: Check all latch and position switches for alignment.

fixSteps.title: Replace faulty sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Replace sensor or module as indicated.

faq.q: Is this a safety issue?
faq.a: No — the roof simply won't operate until repaired.

title: Transmission Control Module Bus A Off
shortDescription: The TCM has gone off the CAN bus.
fullDescription: U0700 indicates the TCM has detected enough bus errors that it has entered bus-off state and stopped transmitting. This isolates the TCM from the network, usually resulting in limp mode.
safetyImpact: Transmission typically enters limp mode — single gear only.

fixSteps.title: Check battery
fixSteps.instruction: Ensure battery voltage is above 12.4V — low voltage causes bus errors.

fixSteps.title: Inspect TCM connector
fixSteps.instruction: Check TCM main connector for water intrusion and bent pins.

fixSteps.title: Replace TCM
fixSteps.instruction: If wiring is good, replace and program TCM.

faq.q: Will this damage the transmission?
faq.a: Limp mode is protective — short-term driving is generally safe but get it fixed.

title: Control Module Communication Bus A Off - TCM
shortDescription: Control module detected TCM has exited the bus.
fullDescription: U0701 is the counterpart to U0700 — stored by another module (ECM, BCM) when the TCM stops responding due to bus-off state. Closely related diagnosis to U0700.
safetyImpact: Transmission in limp mode, potentially unsafe for highway driving.

causes.cause: CAN bus wiring fault near TCM

fixSteps.title: Address U0700
fixSteps.instruction: Diagnose and fix U0700 first — U0701 will usually clear automatically.

fixSteps.title: Clear and retest
fixSteps.instruction: Clear codes and drive cycle to confirm.

faq.q: Do I need to fix U0701 separately?
faq.a: Usually no — fix U0700 and U0701 clears on its own.

title: Invalid Data Received From Transmission Range Sensor
shortDescription: The TCM receives invalid gear position data.
fullDescription: U0728 is set when the TCM receives range sensor data (PRNDL) that does not correspond to a valid gear position. This can cause the transmission to default to a safe gear and disable shifting.
safetyImpact: Transmission may not shift into drive or reverse.

fixSteps.title: Check shift linkage
fixSteps.instruction: Verify linkage is adjusted so the range sensor index matches gear selector.

fixSteps.title: Test range sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Monitor PRNDL live data while moving shifter through gears.

fixSteps.title: Replace range sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the transmission range sensor if data is erratic.

faq.q: Can I drive with this code?
faq.a: No — you may not be able to shift out of park or into reverse safely.

title: Ford SCP Bus Fault - ABS
shortDescription: Ford SCP/UART data bus fault between PCM and ABS module.
fullDescription:
U1039 on Ford applications indicates a loss of communication on the legacy SCP bus between the PCM and the ABS module. Common on older Ford trucks and SUVs before CAN adoption.

Results in speedometer and ABS issues.
safetyImpact: ABS disabled, speedometer may not work.

fixSteps.title: Check SCP bus voltage
fixSteps.instruction: Measure SCP bus voltage and waveform at the DLC.

fixSteps.title: Inspect ABS connectors
fixSteps.instruction: Check ABS module connectors for corrosion.

fixSteps.title: Replace ABS module
fixSteps.instruction: Replace if internal failure confirmed.

faq.q: Is this only on older Fords?
faq.a: Yes, SCP was replaced by CAN by around 2008.

title: Ford SCP - Lost Communication with Engine Coolant Blower Control Module
shortDescription: Ford PCM has lost communication with the cooling fan control module.
fullDescription:
U1073 on Ford applications indicates the PCM cannot communicate with the engine cooling fan controller. The fan may stay off or run continuously.

Common on Taurus, Contour, Mustang with electric cooling fans.
safetyImpact: Engine overheating possible.

fixSteps.title: Check fuses
fixSteps.instruction: Check cooling fan fuses and relays.

fixSteps.title: Inspect connector
fixSteps.instruction: Check fan controller connector on radiator shroud.

fixSteps.title: Replace fan module
fixSteps.instruction: Replace fan controller if faulty.

faq.q: Can I drive with this?
faq.a: Short distances only — watch the temperature gauge closely.

title: Ford SCP - Invalid Data for Body Systems
shortDescription: Ford BCM is receiving invalid data on the SCP network.
fullDescription: U1147 on Ford is a body/comfort data validity fault. Affected features include interior lighting, locks, and wipers.
safetyImpact: Minor comfort feature impact.

fixSteps.title: Check GEM
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect the Generic Electronic Module for water damage.

fixSteps.title: Replace GEM
fixSteps.instruction: If water-damaged, replace and program.

faq.q: Is this common?
faq.a: Yes on older Fords where the GEM sits low and gets wet.

title: Ford SCP - Invalid Data from A/C Control Module
shortDescription: Ford PCM is receiving invalid A/C data on the SCP network.
fullDescription: U1261 on Ford indicates the A/C control module is transmitting but the data is invalid. A/C may not cycle correctly, affecting cooling.
safetyImpact: No safety impact.

fixSteps.title: Scan EATC
fixSteps.instruction: Read codes from the Electronic Automatic Temperature Control module.

fixSteps.title: Check A/C sensors
fixSteps.instruction: Verify A/C pressure and evap temp sensor readings.

fixSteps.title: Replace module
fixSteps.instruction: Replace EATC if module fault confirmed.

faq.q: Why won't my A/C get cold?
faq.a: Without valid pressure data the PCM won't cycle the compressor normally.

title: Ford SCP Communication Bus Fault
shortDescription: Ford-specific SCP communication bus hardware fault.
fullDescription:
U1262 on Ford is set when the PCM or another module detects a fault on the SCP data bus itself — a short, open, or persistent noise. On modern CAN-based Fords, an equivalent U1262 may be stored during intermittent bus glitches.

This is the Ford-specific variant of U1262 (generic version covers different content).
safetyImpact: Multiple modules may lose communication.

fixSteps.title: Measure SCP
fixSteps.instruction: Check SCP pin at DLC for correct idle voltage.

fixSteps.title: Isolate modules
fixSteps.instruction: Disconnect modules one at a time to find the offender.

faq.q: How is this different from generic U1262?
faq.a: Ford's definition pre-dates standardization — the number is identical but the meaning is Ford-specific.

title: Ford - Loss of PATS Communication with BCM
shortDescription: Ford PATS anti-theft system lost communication with BCM.
fullDescription:
U1451 is a common Ford code indicating the Passive Anti-Theft System (PATS) has lost communication with the BCM. Engine will start then stall after 2 seconds.

Classic fault on Focus, Explorer, F-150 with PATS — often caused by transceiver ring failure.
safetyImpact: Engine stalls shortly after start — no-drive condition.
symptoms: Engine starts then stalls 2 seconds later

fixSteps.title: Test with spare
fixSteps.instruction: Try a different programmed key.

fixSteps.title: Inspect transceiver
fixSteps.instruction: Check the PATS antenna ring around the ignition switch for damage.

fixSteps.title: Replace transceiver
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the PATS antenna ring and reprogram keys.

faq.q: Why does my Ford start then stall?
faq.a: Classic PATS fault — engine runs for 2 seconds before anti-theft cuts fuel.

title: GM Class 2 Communication Malfunction
shortDescription: GM Class 2 serial data bus communication fault.
fullDescription:
U1000 on GM vehicles indicates a fault on the Class 2 serial data bus (pre-GMLAN). The bus carries module communication at 10.4 kbps single-wire.

Extremely common GM code on 1996-2007 vehicles — often stored alongside many other U-codes.
safetyImpact: Variable — multiple systems may be affected.
symptoms: No communication with scan tool on some modules

fixSteps.title: Scan all modules
fixSteps.instruction: Identify which modules can't communicate.

fixSteps.title: Check Class 2 voltage
fixSteps.instruction: Pin 2 of DLC should idle around 0V and pulse to 7V during traffic.

fixSteps.title: Isolate modules
fixSteps.instruction: Disconnect modules individually to find the offender.

faq.q: Why do I have 20 U-codes?
faq.a: Class 2 bus failures cause every module to lose communication simultaneously — fix the root cause and most clear.

title: GM - Lost Communication with BCM
shortDescription: GM module has lost communication with the Body Control Module.
fullDescription: U1064 on GM vehicles indicates another module can no longer communicate with the BCM over the Class 2 or GMLAN bus. BCM failures are common, especially in water-intrusion-prone locations.
safetyImpact: Many body features affected — lights, locks, wipers.

fixSteps.title: Inspect BCM
fixSteps.instruction: Locate and inspect BCM for water damage or corrosion.

fixSteps.title: Check power and ground
fixSteps.instruction: Verify BCM has clean 12V and ground.

fixSteps.title: Replace BCM
fixSteps.instruction: Replace and reprogram BCM.

faq.q: Does BCM need programming?
faq.a: Yes — modern GM BCMs must be programmed to the VIN.

title: GM - Lost Communication with Sensing and Diagnostic Module
shortDescription: Lost communication with the SDM (airbag controller).
fullDescription: U1088 on GM vehicles indicates loss of communication with the Sensing and Diagnostic Module (airbag/SRS controller). Airbag warning light illuminates and SRS may be disabled.
safetyImpact: Airbags may not deploy in a crash.

fixSteps.title: Inspect SDM
fixSteps.instruction: Locate SDM under center console and check connector.

fixSteps.title: Check power and ground
fixSteps.instruction: Verify SDM power/ground circuits with ignition on.

fixSteps.title: Replace SDM
fixSteps.instruction: Replace and program SDM — must be VIN-coded.

faq.q: Can I buy a used SDM?
faq.a: No — used SDMs may have crash data locked in and must be replaced new.

title: GM - Lost Communication with IPC
shortDescription: Lost communication with the Instrument Panel Cluster.
fullDescription: U1096 on GM vehicles indicates loss of communication with the Instrument Panel Cluster. Dashboard gauges may not work.
safetyImpact: No speedometer, fuel gauge, or warning displays.

fixSteps.title: Check fuses
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect IPC power fuses.

fixSteps.title: Test cluster
fixSteps.instruction: Check cluster connector pinouts.

fixSteps.title: Replace cluster
fixSteps.instruction: Replace and program cluster with odometer transfer.

faq.q: Is odometer transfer legal?
faq.a: Yes when done properly through GM's SPS programming system.

title: GM - Class 2 Communication Failure
shortDescription: GM Class 2 bus general communication failure.
fullDescription: U1255 is stored when a GM module detects that the Class 2 bus is non-functional for an extended period. Similar to U1000 but more severe — typically indicates hardware failure of the bus.
safetyImpact: Most bus-dependent features fail.

fixSteps.title: Measure bus
fixSteps.instruction: Measure Class 2 pin 2 voltage.

fixSteps.title: Isolate fault
fixSteps.instruction: Disconnect modules to find the short.

faq.q: Is this the same as U1000?
faq.a: Related but U1255 suggests a prolonged bus failure, not intermittent.

title: GM - Class 2 Short to Ground
shortDescription: Class 2 data line shorted to ground.
fullDescription: U1300 on GM indicates the Class 2 communication wire is shorted to ground, pulling the bus low. No modules can communicate.
safetyImpact: Vehicle may not start or run poorly.

fixSteps.title: Measure bus resistance
fixSteps.instruction: Check resistance from pin 2 to ground with key off; should be high.

fixSteps.title: Trace wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Follow Class 2 wiring for damage.

faq.q: Can a module cause a short?
faq.a: Yes — a failed module can short the bus internally.

title: GM - Class 2 Short to Battery Voltage
shortDescription: Class 2 data line shorted to battery voltage.
fullDescription: U1301 on GM indicates the Class 2 wire is shorted to 12V. Modules cannot pull the line low to transmit, so communication fails.
safetyImpact: Wide-ranging module communication failure.

fixSteps.title: Measure voltage
fixSteps.instruction: With ignition on, Class 2 should idle near 0V. If at 12V it's shorted to power.

fixSteps.title: Isolate fault
fixSteps.instruction: Find the damaged wire or offending module.

faq.q: Can this happen from a rodent chew?
faq.a: Yes — rodent damage commonly shorts bus wires to adjacent power wires.

title: Chrysler - Implausible Fuel Level Signal Received
shortDescription: Chrysler PCM received an impossible fuel level reading.
fullDescription: U1403 on Chrysler indicates the PCM received fuel level data from the BCM that is implausible (e.g., jumping more than possible in a short time). May affect evap monitor readiness.
safetyImpact: No direct safety impact.

fixSteps.title: Monitor fuel level data
fixSteps.instruction: Watch live data from PCM and BCM for jumps.

fixSteps.title: Replace sending unit
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the fuel level sending unit in the tank.

faq.q: Is fuel gauge affected?
faq.a: Yes — same sensor feeds both PCM and dashboard.

title: Chrysler - Implausible Evap Emission System Leak Detection Signal
shortDescription: Evap leak detection data is implausible.
fullDescription: U1411 on Chrysler means the PCM received evap leak detection pump or pressure data that makes no physical sense. Sets as part of evap diagnostic routine.
safetyImpact: Emissions impact only.

fixSteps.title: Test NVLD
fixSteps.instruction: Test Natural Vacuum Leak Detection module operation.

fixSteps.title: Replace NVLD
fixSteps.instruction: Replace NVLD module if faulty.

faq.q: What is NVLD?
faq.a: Chrysler's Natural Vacuum Leak Detection module replaces traditional evap purge/vent valves.

title: Chrysler - Implausible Vehicle Speed Signal Received
shortDescription: Vehicle speed signal is implausible.
fullDescription: U1412 is Chrysler-specific code where PCM receives VSS data that jumps impossibly. Often caused by ABS module or wheel speed sensor faults, since VSS data is bus-shared.
safetyImpact: Cruise control may disable; transmission shift quality affected.

fixSteps.title: Scan ABS
fixSteps.instruction: Read ABS codes for wheel speed sensor faults.

fixSteps.title: Inspect sensors
fixSteps.instruction: Check wheel speed sensors for damage.

fixSteps.title: Replace faulty sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Replace as needed.

faq.q: Why does my speedo jump?
faq.a: A bad wheel sensor gives random pulses that the ABS shares with the PCM.

title: Chrysler - Implausible Left/Right Wheel Distance Signal
shortDescription: Left/right wheel distance ratio is implausible.
fullDescription: U1417 on Chrysler is set when wheel speed sensors report distances that don't match up as expected for straight-line driving. Indicates a failing wheel sensor or tone ring.
safetyImpact: ABS and traction control may be affected.

fixSteps.title: Inspect tone rings
fixSteps.instruction: Check tone rings at each wheel for missing teeth or debris.

fixSteps.title: Measure air gap
fixSteps.instruction: Verify WSS air gap is within spec.

faq.q: Can I drive with this code?
faq.a: Yes, but with ABS degraded — avoid hard braking.

title: Mazda - CAN Communication Bus Fault
shortDescription: Mazda-specific CAN bus generic fault.
fullDescription:
U1900 on Mazda vehicles indicates a CAN communication fault — the Mazda-specific equivalent to generic U0001. Commonly stored with many companion U-codes.

Fixing the underlying wiring or module issue clears the code across modules.
safetyImpact: Wide-ranging — multiple systems.

fixSteps.title: Check battery
fixSteps.instruction: Load test battery — Mazda CAN is voltage-sensitive.

fixSteps.title: Measure CAN
fixSteps.instruction: Verify CAN-H/CAN-L termination resistance of 60 ohms.

faq.q: Why is this code different on Mazda?
faq.a: Mazda uses U1900 as a manufacturer-specific CAN fault alongside generic codes.

title: Mazda - Fault Received From External Node
shortDescription: Mazda module received fault notification from another node.
fullDescription: U2023 on Mazda is stored when a receiving module is informed by another node that it has an active DTC — essentially a propagation marker. Address the root fault in the sending module.
safetyImpact: Depends on source fault.

fixSteps.title: Scan all modules
fixSteps.instruction: Identify the source of the actual fault across the network.

fixSteps.title: Fix root cause
fixSteps.instruction: Repair the triggering module's DTC.

faq.q: Can I clear this alone?
faq.a: No — fix the originating DTC first.

title: Mazda - Initial Configuration Not Complete
shortDescription: Mazda module initial configuration is incomplete.
fullDescription: U2100 is stored when a Mazda module (often a replacement) has not been fully configured or programmed for the vehicle. Typically appears after a module swap.
safetyImpact: Features dependent on the module will be inoperative.

fixSteps.title: Perform module configuration
fixSteps.instruction: Use Mazda IDS or equivalent to complete module programming.

faq.q: Can I do this at home?
faq.a: Only with an IDS or J2534 pass-thru subscription to Mazda.

title: Nissan - CAN Communication Circuit
shortDescription: Nissan-specific CAN communication fault.
fullDescription:
U1000 on Nissan is a CAN communication line fault — equivalent to generic U0001 but set by Nissan ECMs and TCMs when they detect CAN errors. Very common on Altima, Maxima, Sentra.

Often triggered by a failing body module or corroded chassis grounds.
safetyImpact: Transmission may enter fail-safe mode.

fixSteps.title: Clean grounds
fixSteps.instruction: Clean main chassis and engine grounds — Nissan U1000 often clears after ground refresh.

fixSteps.title: Check CAN
fixSteps.instruction: Verify 60 ohm resistance between CAN-H and CAN-L with key off.

faq.q: Why is my Nissan in fail-safe?
faq.a: Nissan TCMs go to fail-safe on CAN errors; fix U1000 root cause to restore shifting.

title: Nissan - CAN Communication Line Circuit Malfunction
shortDescription: Nissan CAN communication line hardware malfunction.
fullDescription: U1001 on Nissan is a harder-failure variant of U1000 — specifically flagging that the CAN line itself has a persistent hardware malfunction, not just intermittent errors.
safetyImpact: Vehicle may be undriveable.

fixSteps.title: Measure termination
fixSteps.instruction: Should read ~60 ohms across CAN-H and CAN-L with key off.

fixSteps.title: Repair wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Locate and repair the open or short.

faq.q: Is this the same as U1000?
faq.a: No — U1001 indicates a confirmed hardware issue, not just bus errors.

title: Control Module Configuration Memory Corrupted
shortDescription: Control module configuration data is corrupted.
fullDescription: U2008 indicates the module has detected corruption in its VIN or option configuration memory. Features may default to base configuration.
safetyImpact: Features may be disabled or incorrect.

fixSteps.title: Reprogram module
fixSteps.instruction: Perform a full module reflash with factory software.

faq.q: Do I need to replace the module?
faq.a: Usually a reflash fixes it — replacement only if flash cannot be written.

title: CAN Initialization Failure - ECM
shortDescription: ECM CAN controller failed to initialize at startup.
fullDescription: U2104 is set when the ECM's CAN controller cannot initialize communication at power-up. Usually indicates an internal ECM fault or major wiring issue at the ECM connector.
safetyImpact: Engine may not start.

fixSteps.title: Check ECM connector
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect ECM main connectors for corrosion or bent pins.

fixSteps.title: Replace ECM
fixSteps.instruction: Replace ECM if internal fault confirmed.

faq.q: Can a bad battery cause this?
faq.a: A very low battery can prevent ECM boot; check battery first.

title: CAN Initialization Failure - TCM
shortDescription: TCM CAN controller failed to initialize.
fullDescription: U2105 indicates the TCM couldn't initialize its CAN hardware. TCM will go offline — transmission will enter limp mode.
safetyImpact: Transmission limp mode.

fixSteps.title: Inspect TCM
fixSteps.instruction: Check TCM connector for damage.

fixSteps.title: Replace TCM
fixSteps.instruction: Replace and program TCM.

faq.q: What's limp mode like?
faq.a: Usually stuck in second or third gear, no upshifts.

title: CAN Initialization Failure - BCM
shortDescription: BCM CAN controller failed to initialize.
fullDescription: U2107 indicates the BCM cannot initialize CAN communications at startup. Body features and inter-module communication fail.
safetyImpact: Body features inoperative.

fixSteps.title: Inspect BCM
fixSteps.instruction: Check BCM for water damage.

fixSteps.title: Replace BCM
fixSteps.instruction: Replace and program.

faq.q: Is BCM replacement expensive?
faq.a: Moderately — programming is often the bulk of the cost.

title: VIN Does Not Match Between Control Modules
shortDescription: One or more modules have mismatched VIN programming.
fullDescription: U2138 is set when the receiving module sees a VIN value from another module that does not match its own. Commonly occurs after a module replacement where VIN programming was skipped.
safetyImpact: Features may be disabled.

causes.cause: Replacement module not programmed to VIN

fixSteps.title: Program VIN
fixSteps.instruction: Write the correct VIN to the offending module.

faq.q: Why does the VIN matter?
faq.a: Modules validate VIN for anti-theft and feature enablement.

title: Control Module Configuration Data Not Programmed
shortDescription: Module configuration data has not been programmed.
fullDescription: U2200 is set when a module is installed but its configuration data (vehicle options, coding) has not been written. Typically after new module installation.
safetyImpact: Features default to base or disabled.

fixSteps.title: Configure module
fixSteps.instruction: Use factory scan tool to write configuration data.

faq.q: Who can configure modules?
faq.a: Dealer or independent shops with factory-level scan tools.
